board/emulator integration polish

That is enough for a disciplined implementation roadmap.

3. Earliest runtime split is sufficiently specified
The agent can proceed with this split:

Assembly first
crt0.S

stack init

BSS/data init if needed

jump to main

optional putchar/getchar/halt

C first
value representation

heap

symbol table

env

eval

read

print

builtins

main/test harness

That is concrete enough to start coding.

4. Smallest subset is sufficiently specified
The agent can implement the first milestone as:

Data
fixnums

symbols

cons cells

nil

Special forms
quote

if

define

lambda

Builtins
cons

car

cdr

eq?

atom?

+ - * <

print

Reader
integers

symbols

lists

'x

That is a real, actionable MVP.

5. Project intent is sufficiently specified
The agent knows this is for:

learning language implementation

validating toolchain quality

exercising compiler/assembler/emulator on non-trivial software

eventually enabling embedded apps in Lisp

That helps it make the right tradeoffs toward simplicity and observability rather than premature sophistication.

What is not sufficiently specified yet
This is the important part. An agent can start, but these gaps matter.

1. COR24 ABI / calling convention
This is the biggest missing piece.

The agent needs to know things like:

how arguments are passed

return value register(s)

caller-saved vs callee-saved registers

stack growth direction

alignment rules

how C and assembly interoperate

whether function pointers work normally

how structs are returned/passed

whether recursion is reliable with your C compiler

Without this, an agent can still write portable C, but target-specific asm glue will be guesswork.

2. Memory map and startup model
The agent needs:

RAM start/end

ROM/flash layout if relevant

stack location/size

heap location/size

whether .data must be copied manually

whether .bss must be zeroed manually

memory-mapped UART addresses if used

emulator I/O model

Without this, it can only write placeholder startup code.

3. Exact assembler syntax and constraints
Assemblers vary a lot.

The agent needs:

assembler syntax flavor

directives for sections, globals, constants, data allocation

comment syntax

macro syntax if any

calling/jump syntax

object/output format expectations

linker expectations or load format

Without that, it can only produce pseudo-assembly or tentative assembly.

4. C compiler capabilities and limitations
This matters a lot for a Lisp.

The agent needs to know:

which C standard is supported, roughly

whether stdint.h-style types exist

whether static, const, enum, function pointers, recursion, and local arrays work

whether bitfields or shifts on 24-bit-ish values are reliable

whether switch works well

whether malloc exists or is irrelevant

whether long is 24 or 32 bits

whether char* string literals work normally

how globals/statics are laid out

whether there are known compiler bugs

Without

Streaming interrupted. Waiting for the complete message...


continue

Restating the requirement
You are asking whether the discussion so far is sufficient for an AI coding agent to start implementing a bootstrap Lisp for COR24 using your existing C compiler, assembler, and emulator, or whether more concrete documentation is still needed.

The answer is:

Yes — enough to start a planning + scaffold + host-side prototype exists.
No — not enough yet for a reliable target-execution implementation without the missing machine/toolchain details.
So an agent can proceed partway right now, but only if it is constrained to the right initial scope.

What an agent can safely do now
With what is already documented, an agent can already create:

1. The project structure
It can lay out a repo like:

docs/

requirements.md

architecture.md

bootstrap-plan.md

memory-model.md

test-plan.md

src/

value.h

heap.c

symbol.c

env.c

eval.c

read.c

print.c

builtin.c

main.c

asm/

crt0.S

board_io.S

tests/

reader tests

eval tests

GC tests

Lisp script tests

2. A host-side reference implementation
An agent can implement the first bootstrap version in portable C for normal execution on a desktop or host.

That is already well specified enough because we have agreed on:

tiny Lisp

Lisp-1

lexical scope

CL-style macros later

mark-sweep GC later

initial no-GC phase

minimal special forms and primitives

staged bootstrap order

3. A target-independent language core
An agent can write:

value tagging API

symbol table

environment model

evaluator

basic reader/printer

builtins

early tests

4. Documentation
An agent can also generate the missing design documents from the decisions already made.

That is actually a good next step because it converts conversational intent into executable engineering constraints.

What an agent cannot reliably do yet
For actual COR24 bring-up, the current discussion is not yet sufficient in the low-level sense.

The missing information is not about Lisp semantics anymore; it is about the target contract.

An agent still lacks enough detail to confidently write working target-specific code for:

1. ABI / calling convention
It needs to know:

argument passing rules

return value rules

callee-saved vs caller-saved registers

stack growth direction

stack alignment

frame-pointer conventions

varargs support, if any

how C and asm interoperate on COR24

Without this, assembly stubs and low-level runtime glue are guesswork.

2. Memory map
It needs:

RAM start/end

ROM/flash start/end

MMIO regions

UART addresses

boot/reset vector locations

whether code and data live in separate spaces

whether writable .data must be copied from ROM to RAM

3. Object-size assumptions in your C compiler
It needs:

size of char, short, int, long, pointer

signedness defaults

struct layout/alignment rules

enum size

bitfield behavior if any

endianness

This matters a lot for tagged values, heap structs, and GC scanning.

4. C runtime support
It needs to know whether the compiler/linker already provides:

crt0

stack initialization

.bss clearing

.data relocation

memcpy, memset

division/mod helpers

malloc stubs or no runtime at all

If not, those must be written explicitly.

5. Assembler syntax and linker format
It needs:

assembler dialect

symbol declaration syntax

section syntax

relocation capabilities

linker script format

how to declare reset vectors / entry point

how to link C output with handwritten assembly

6. Emulator control surface
It needs:

how to load binaries

how to run headless tests

how to capture stdout/console

how to inspect memory/registers

how to automate regression tests

whether UART/MMIO is emulated exactly like hardware

7. Failure/debugging workflow
It needs:

how assertions should fail

whether there is a trap instruction

how to breakpoint

how to dump heap state

how to extract logs from emulator runs

So is there “enough documented here”?
Enough for:
architecture

repo setup

staged implementation plan

host-side bootstrap interpreter

test scaffolding

target-abstraction interfaces

documentation generation

Not enough for:
actual COR24 crt0

board I/O layer

linker script

exact tagged layout tied to machine word behavior

assembly helper routines

automated target test execution

So the right answer is:

There is enough here for an AI coding agent to begin productively, but not enough to finish a working COR24 port without a target-spec handoff.
Best way to use an agent right now
The agent should be told to proceed in two bounded phases.

Phase 1 — proceed now
Have the agent implement:

Documents
requirements.md

architecture.md

bootstrap-plan.md

cor24-open-questions.md

Portable implementation
minimal C interpreter

no-GC first

reader/printer

eval

environments

tests

host runner

Target abstraction layer
Define interfaces like:

platform_putchar(int ch)

platform_getchar(void)

platform_halt(int code)

platform_heap_start()

platform_heap_end()

Stub these on host first.

That work is safe and useful immediately.

Phase 2 — only after target handoff
Then give the agent a small precise hardware/toolchain packet, and have it implement:

crt0.S

linker script

board I/O

target build system

emulator test harness

heap placement

low-level debug support

What exact information should you provide next?
The minimum handoff packet should contain these files or notes.

A. COR24 machine note
register set

pointer width

endianness

alignment rules

stack convention

call/return convention

B. C compiler note
sizes of scalar types

example generated assembly for a tiny C function

how globals/statics are emitted

any unsupported C features

C. Assembler/linker note
sample assembly file

directives for sections, globals, constants

how to link with C objects

how entry point is selected

D. Emulator note
load/run command

capture output command

exit status behavior

debug/breakpoint support

E. Board or emulator MMIO note
UART TX address

UART RX address

status register semantics

halt or exit mechanism

A very practical threshold test
Here is the simplest test for whether an AI agent has enough to proceed to target work:

Can it write and successfully run this program on COR24?

C
main() calls putchar('O'), putchar('K'), newline

Assembly/runtime
startup enters main

UART output works

binary loads in emulator

output is captured automatically

If that is not yet fully specified, then target bring-up is not yet agent-ready.

If that is specified, then the agent can start doing useful COR24 work.

Recommended project split for the agent
I would explicitly instruct the agent to create these milestones.

Milestone 1
Portable host-only Lisp core:

fixnums

symbols

cons

reader

printer

global env

quote, if, define, lambda

arithmetic/list primitives

Milestone 2
Lexical closures and tests.

Milestone 3
GC.

Milestone 4
Macros.

Milestone 5
COR24 platform layer once target packet is supplied.

That keeps the agent from wandering into underspecified areas.

What I would tell the agent, in plain terms
A good instruction would be:

Implement a host-side bootstrap Lisp in portable C first. Do not assume any COR24 ABI details yet. Isolate all platform dependencies behind a tiny platform interface. Generate documentation for the missing target-specific information needed before porting to COR24.

That is enough to get strong progress immediately.

Bottom line
Yes:
There is enough documented for an AI coding agent to start now on the language core, tests, repository structure, and host-side implementation.

No:
There is not enough documented yet for the agent to reliably complete the COR24-specific runtime and assembly glue without a short, explicit target/toolchain specification.

The next best step is to turn this conversation into a compact engineering handoff: one requirements doc, one architecture doc, and one COR24/toolchain-spec questionnaire.

content.text:
I've read everything. Here's a summary:

**CLAUDE.md** — Project config: Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c), a minimal Lisp-1 with lexical scope, unhygienic defmacro, closures, and mark-sweep GC targeting COR24 24-bit RISC. Uses AgentRail for workflow. Build with `make`, strict `-Wall -Wextra -Werror -std=c11`.

**docs/research.txt** — Extensive design research covering:
- **Language choices**: Lisp-1, lexical scope, CL-style unhygienic `defmacro`, quasiquote + gensym
- **Implementation options**: Favors porting/adapting Robert van Engelen's small C Lisp; femtolisp as design reference; uLisp for embedded ideas
- **Bootstrap order**: (1) no-GC evaluator → (2) reader/printer → (3) lexical closures → (4) mark-sweep GC → (5) macros → (6) strings → (7) board port
- **Runtime**: Tagged 24-bit words, single-heap mark-sweep GC, cons/symbol/string/closure/primitive/macro object types
- **Minimal subset**: fixnums, symbols, cons, quote/if/define/lambda, car/cdr/cons/eq?/atom?/arithmetic/print
- **Phase 1 scope**: Host-side portable C first, COR24 asm/board later (ABI, memory map, assembler syntax still need specification)

Want me to run `agentrail next` to see where the project stands?

message.content: yes, but also let me know what you will do

=== PLAN ===
Build a Tiny Macro Lisp system that compiles to COR24 assembly. Phase 1: lexer and parser. Phase 2: evaluator with core special forms. Phase 3: compiler targeting COR24 assembly. Phase 4: integration with tc24r and cor24-rs toolchain.

=== STEPS ===
  [!] 001-lexer [production]: Implement the Lisp lexer: tokenize parentheses, symbols, numbers, strings, an...
  [ ] 002-research-and-plan [production]: Read research, study COR24 toolchain, create architecture/prd/design/plan/stats docs <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 003-scaffold [production]: Set up C source structure based on architecture
  [ ] 004-lexer [production]: Implement tokenizer for Lisp syntax
  [ ] 005-reader [production]: Implement S-expression reader
  [ ] 006-eval-core [production]: Implement eval with core special forms
  [ ] 007-gc [production]: Implement mark-sweep garbage collector
  [ ] 008-compiler [production]: Compile to COR24 assembly
  [ ] 009-integration [production]: Wire to tc24r and cor24-rs toolchain

=== CURRENT STEP: 002-research-and-plan [production] ===
Status: pending
Description: Read research, study COR24 toolchain, create architecture/prd/design/plan/stats docs

Context files:
  - docs/research.txt

=== PROMPT ===
Read docs/research.txt (3000 lines of Lisp design research for COR24).

Study the COR24 toolchain:
- ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs -- COR24 assembler and emulator (read its README, ISA docs)
- ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r -- Tiny COR24 compiler in Rust (read its README, architecture)

Then create these documentation files:

1. docs/architecture.md -- System overview: components (reader, evaluator, compiler, GC, runtime), memory model (24-bit address space, heap layout, stack), how they connect. Component diagram.

2. docs/prd.md -- Product requirements: target demos (anaphoric macros, list processing, arithmetic, strings), constraints (COR24 24-bit ISA, embedded-class memory), success criteria.

3. docs/design.md -- Detailed design: data representation (tagged values in 24-bit words), cell layout, environment structure, closure representation, GC algorithm (mark-sweep), special forms list, macro expansion strategy.

4. docs/plan.md -- Phased implementation plan with concrete steps. Phase 1: reader/evaluator in C. Phase 2: core library in Lisp. Phase 3: compiler to COR24 assembly. Phase 4: integration and demos.

5. docs/stats.md -- COR24 ISA summary relevant to Lisp: instruction set, register count, addressing modes, memory size, what operations are native vs need runtime support.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

1→# tc24r -- Tiny C Compiler for the COR24 FPGA Soft CPU
     2→
     3→tc24r is an open-source C compiler targeting the **COR24** (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit) instruction set architecture. COR24 is an FPGA soft CPU designed by [MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) for efficient C code execution. The existing MakerLisp C compiler (cc24) is proprietary -- tc24r provides an open-source alternative.
     4→
     5→The approach is inspired by [chibicc](https://github.com/rui314/chibicc), a small educational C compiler. tc24r follows the same architecture pattern (recursive-descent parser, direct assembly emission) but is written from scratch in Rust.
     6→
     7→## Project Status
     8→
     9→tc24r is **functional** -- it compiles real C programs to COR24 assembly that runs on hardware and the cor24-rs emulator. 14 components, ~50 crates, 45 demos all passing. See [docs/status.md](docs/status.md) for detailed status and test counts.
    10→
    11→### What Works
    12→
    13→- Types: int (24-bit), char (8-bit), void, pointers, arrays
    14→- All standard C operators with correct precedence
    15→- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case
    16→- Ternary operator (? :), character literals, multi-declaration
    17→- Prefix/postfix increment and decrement (++i, i--)
    18→- Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=
    19→- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct (dot and arrow access), union
    20→- Function prototypes (forward declarations, mutual recursion)
    21→- Functions with multiple parameters, recursion, ISR support
    22→- Globals, string constants, hex literals
    23→- Pointer arithmetic with element-size scaling
    24→- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
    25→- Inline assembly: asm("...")
    26→- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt enable
    27→- Software division and modulo runtime
    28→
    29→### What Does Not Work Yet
    30→
    31→- Multi-file compilation
    32→- float/double (COR24 has no FPU -- out of scope)
    33→
    34→## COR24 Architecture at a Glance
    35→
    36→- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
    37→- **8 registers**: r0-r2 (general purpose), fp, sp, z (zero), iv, ir
    38→- **32 instructions**: arithmetic, logic, shift, compare, branch, load/store, stack ops
    39→- **1/2/4-byte** variable-length instruction encoding
    40→- Hardware multiply (24-cycle), no hardware divide
    41→- Little-endian, byte-addressable memory
    42→- UART and GPIO via memory-mapped I/O
    43→
    44→## Component Structure
    45→
    46→The compiler is organized into 14 components with ~50 crates, each component
    47→being its own Cargo workspace under `components/`:
    48→
    49→| Component | Purpose |
    50→|-----------|---------|
    51→| core | Shared types: AST, tokens, spans, errors, traits |
    52→| frontend | Preprocessing, lexing, parsing |
    53→| backend | Legacy codegen and validation |
    54→| codegen-emit | Assembly emission (core, data, load/store) |
    55→| codegen-expr | Expression codegen (call, literal, ops, pointer, variable) |
    56→| codegen-ops | Operator codegen (arithmetic, bitwise, compare, divmod, incdec, logical, unary, type-infer) |
    57→| codegen-state | Codegen state management |
    58→| codegen-stmt | Statement codegen (control flow, simple statements) |
    59→| codegen-structure | Function structure (ISR, locals, prologue) |
    60→| config | Configuration and target definition |
    61→| dispatch | Codegen dispatch layer |
    62→| macros | Proc macros (asm-dsl, emit-macros, handler-macros) |
    63→| testing | Test harnesses (as24, compile, cor24, golden) |
    64→| cli | CLI binary entry point |
    65→
    66→See [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) for data flow and design constraints.
    67→
    68→## Building
    69→
    70→Build all components:
    71→
    72→```bash
    73→scripts/build-all.sh
    74→```
    75→
    76→This runs each component's `scripts/build.sh` in dependency order. The release binary is produced at:
    77→
    78→```
    79→components/cli/target/release/tc24r
    80→```
    81→
    82→To build a single component:
    83→
    84→```bash
    85→cargo build --manifest-path components/<name>/Cargo.toml
    86→```
    87→
    88→## Usage
    89→
    90→```bash
    91→tc24r <input.c> [-o output.s] [-I dir]
    92→```
    93→
    94→- `<input.c>` -- C source file to compile
    95→- `-o output.s` -- output assembly file (default: stdout)
    96→- `-I dir` -- add include search path (repeatable)
    97→
    98→### Full Workflow
    99→
   100→```bash
   101→# Compile C to COR24 assembly
   102→tc24r program.c -o program.s
   103→
   104→# Assemble (requires as24 from COR24-TB archive)
   105→as24 < program.s | longlgo > program.lgo
   106→
   107→# Run on emulator (cor24-rs)
   108→cd ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs
   109→cargo run -- run path/to/program.s
   110→```

1→# COR24 C Data Model
     2→
     3→Type sizes, alignment, signedness, and pointer model for the tc24r compiler.
     4→
     5→## Type Sizes
     6→
     7→| C Type | Size (bits) | Size (bytes) | Signedness | Notes |
     8→|--------|-------------|-------------|------------|-------|
     9→| `char` | 8 | 1 | signed | Matches C default; use `unsigned char` for unsigned |
    10→| `unsigned char` | 8 | 1 | unsigned | |
    11→| `short` | 16 | 2 | signed | Stored as 2 bytes in memory |
    12→| `unsigned short` | 16 | 2 | unsigned | |
    13→| `int` | 24 | 3 | signed | Native machine word |
    14→| `unsigned int` | 24 | 3 | unsigned | |
    15→| `long` | 24 | 3 | signed | Same as int for initial version |
    16→| `unsigned long` | 24 | 3 | unsigned | |
    17→| `long long` | - | - | - | Not supported initially |
    18→| `float` | - | - | - | Not supported initially |
    19→| `double` | - | - | - | Not supported initially |
    20→| `_Bool` | 8 | 1 | unsigned | 0 or 1 |
    21→| pointer | 24 | 3 | - | Same as int; full address space |
    22→| `enum` | 24 | 3 | signed | Same as int |
    23→
    24→### Rationale for 24-bit int
    25→
    26→COR24 is a 24-bit machine. Making `int` 24 bits:
    27→- Maps naturally to the hardware word size
    28→- Avoids expensive multi-word arithmetic for every int operation
    29→- Matches pointer size (simplifies pointer/int conversions)
    30→- Is honest about the machine's capabilities
    31→
    32→The MakerLisp C compiler uses this same model.
    33→
    34→### What About 32-bit int?
    35→
    36→A 32-bit int would require:
    37→- Every arithmetic operation to use two registers or helper routines
    38→- Load/store pairs for every int access
    39→- Significant codegen complexity for a demo compiler
    40→
    41→This could be revisited later, but for tc24r v1, 24-bit int is correct.
    42→
    43→## Alignment
    44→
    45→| Type | Alignment |
    46→|------|-----------|
    47→| `char` | 1 byte |
    48→| `short` | 1 byte (no alignment requirement) |
    49→| `int` / pointer | 3 bytes (word-aligned preferred) |
    50→| struct members | Natural alignment of each member |
    51→
    52→COR24 hardware supports unaligned word access, but aligned access is more
    53→efficient. The compiler should emit word-aligned locals and globals where
    54→practical.
    55→
    56→Stack slots are always 3-byte aligned (the stack operates in 24-bit words).
    57→
    58→## Endianness
    59→
    60→**Little-endian.** Least significant byte at lowest address.
    61→
    62→A 24-bit value 0x123456 stored at address A:
    63→- A+0: 0x56 (low byte)
    64→- A+1: 0x34 (mid byte)
    65→- A+2: 0x12 (high byte)
    66→
    67→This matches `lw`/`sw` instruction behavior.
    68→
    69→## Integer Promotions
    70→
    71→For the initial compiler, integer promotion rules are simplified:
    72→
    73→1. `char` is promoted to `int` (24-bit) in expressions.
    74→2. `short` is promoted to `int` (24-bit) in expressions.
    75→3. Operations produce `int` (24-bit) results.
    76→4. When storing back to `char`, truncate to low 8 bits.
    77→5. When storing back to `short`, truncate to low 16 bits.
    78→
    79→Sign extension / zero extension is handled by:
    80→- `sxt ra,rb` -- sign-extend byte to 24-bit
    81→- `zxt ra,rb` -- zero-extend byte to 24-bit
    82→- `lb` -- load byte with sign extension
    83→- `lbu` -- load byte with zero extension
    84→
    85→For `short`, there are no 16-bit load/store instructions. Use:
    86→- Load short: `lw` then mask/sign-extend (or two `lb`/`lbu` instructions)
    87→- Store short: `sb` low byte, shift right 8, `sb` high byte
    88→
    89→### Practical Note on short
    90→
    91→Given the added complexity of 16-bit types on a 24-bit machine with no
    92→native 16-bit load/store, the initial compiler may treat `short` as
    93→equivalent to `int` (24-bit) for simplicity. This is a pragmatic choice
    94→for a demo compiler.
    95→
    96→## Pointer Representation
    97→
    98→- All pointers are 24 bits (3 bytes).
    99→- Code and data share a single flat address space.
   100→- Function pointers and data pointers have the same representation.
   101→- `sizeof(void *)` == `sizeof(int)` == 3.
   102→- NULL pointer is 0x000000.
   103→
   104→## sizeof Summary
   105→
   106→| Expression | Value |
   107→|-----------|-------|
   108→| `sizeof(char)` | 1 |
   109→| `sizeof(short)` | 2 |
   110→| `sizeof(int)` | 3 |
   111→| `sizeof(long)` | 3 |
   112→| `sizeof(void *)` | 3 |
   113→| `sizeof(int *)` | 3 |
   114→
   115→## Array Layout
   116→
   117→Arrays are laid out contiguously in memory with elements at stride
   118→`sizeof(element)`:
   119→
   120→```
   121→int arr[4]:     arr+0, arr+3, arr+6, arr+9  (stride 3)
   122→char buf[8]:    buf+0, buf+1, buf+2, ...     (stride 1)
   123→```
   124→
   125→Pointer arithmetic scales by element size:
   126→- `int *p; p+1` advances by 3 bytes
   127→- `char *p; p+1` advances by 1 byte
   128→
   129→## Struct Layout (Deferred)
   130→
   131→Structs are not supported in the initial compiler version. When added:
   132→- Members laid out in declaration order
   133→- Each member at its natural alignment
   134→- Total struct size padded to alignment of largest member
   135→- Passed by pointer, returned via static storage pointer in r0
   136→
   137→## Limits
   138→
   139→| Constant | Value | Hex |
   140→|----------|-------|-----|
   141→| `CHAR_BIT` | 8 | |
   142→| `CHAR_MIN` | -128 | 0x80 |
   143→| `CHAR_MAX` | 127 | 0x7F |
   144→| `UCHAR_MAX` | 255 | 0xFF |
   145→| `INT_MIN` | -8388608 | 0x800000 |
   146→| `INT_MAX` | 8388607 | 0x7FFFFF |
   147→| `UINT_MAX` | 16777215 | 0xFFFFFF |
   148→| `LONG_MIN` | -8388608 | 0x800000 |
   149→| `LONG_MAX` | 8388607 | 0x7FFFFF |
   150→
   151→## Features Deferred
   152→
   153→The following are not supported in tc24r v1:
   154→
   155→- `long long` (would require 48-bit multi-word arithmetic)
   156→- `float`, `double` (no FPU; would need software emulation)
   157→- Bitfields
   158→- `_Complex`
   159→- Variable-length arrays
   160→- `volatile` semantics (all memory accesses are currently volatile-like)
   161→

1→# COR24 C ABI Proposal (v0)
     2→
     3→This ABI is based on MakerLisp's proprietary COR24 C compiler conventions,
     4→as described in direct feedback from the COR24 hardware designer and
     5→confirmed by examining MakerLisp compiler output (.s files from fib.c,
     6→sieve.c, etc.).
     7→
     8→## Register Roles
     9→
    10→| Register | Saved By | Role |
    11→|----------|----------|------|
    12→| r0 | Caller (not saved) | Return value, scratch, first temp |
    13→| r1 | Callee | Return address (from `jal`) |
    14→| r2 | Callee | Register variable (preserved across calls) |
    15→| fp (r3) | Callee | Frame pointer |
    16→| sp (r4) | - | Stack pointer (maintained by convention) |
    17→| z (r5) | - | Zero constant (read-only) |
    18→| iv (r6) | - | Interrupt vector (set by startup code) |
    19→| ir (r7) | - | Interrupt return (set by CPU) |
    20→
    21→### Key Points
    22→
    23→- **r0** is freely clobbered by any function. Used for return values.
    24→- **r1** holds the return address after `jal`. Callee must save/restore it
    25→  if the function makes any calls (non-leaf).
    26→- **r2** is the only general-purpose callee-saved register. Use it as a
    27→  "register variable" -- it survives across function calls.
    28→- **fp** is always saved/restored. Used to access arguments and locals.
    29→
    30→## Argument Passing
    31→
    32→Arguments are passed **on the stack**, pushed **right-to-left** before the
    33→call. The caller cleans up arguments after the call returns.
    34→
    35→```asm
    36→; calling func(a, b, c):
    37→        push    c_value         ; arg 3 (rightmost, pushed first)
    38→        push    b_value         ; arg 2
    39→        push    a_value         ; arg 1 (leftmost, pushed last)
    40→        la      r0,_func
    41→        jal     r1,(r0)         ; call
    42→        add     sp,9            ; clean up 3 args (3 bytes each)
    43→```
    44→
    45→Each argument occupies one 24-bit stack slot (3 bytes), regardless of the
    46→C type. `char` and `short` values are widened to 24 bits before pushing.
    47→
    48→### Accessing Arguments in Callee
    49→
    50→After the standard prologue, arguments are at positive offsets from fp:
    51→
    52→```
    53→        lw      r0,9(fp)        ; first argument
    54→        lw      r0,12(fp)       ; second argument
    55→        lw      r0,15(fp)       ; third argument
    56→        ; general: arg N is at fp + 9 + (N-1)*3
    57→```
    58→
    59→The offset of 9 accounts for the three saved registers (fp, r2, r1) that
    60→the prologue pushes before setting fp = sp.
    61→
    62→### Byte-Sized Arguments
    63→
    64→When a `char` argument is passed, it occupies a full 24-bit stack slot.
    65→The callee loads it with `lb` (sign-extended) or `lbu` (zero-extended)
    66→at the appropriate byte offset within the slot:
    67→
    68→```asm
    69→        lb      r0,9(fp)        ; load char argument (sign-extended)
    70→```
    71→
    72→Note: The MakerLisp compiler loads `char` args with `lb` at the base of
    73→the slot (byte 0 of the 3-byte word), relying on little-endian layout
    74→where the low byte is at the lowest address.
    75→
    76→## Return Values
    77→
    78→- **char or int (24-bit):** returned directly in r0.
    79→- **Larger types (long, float, double, structs):** r0 contains a pointer
    80→  to a function-specific static storage area in .bss. The caller must copy
    81→  the value out before calling the function again, as the next call
    82→  overwrites the return area. (This follows the Portable C Compiler model.)
    83→
    84→For the initial tc24r compiler (demo subset), only `char` and `int` return
    85→values need to be supported -- both directly in r0.
    86→
    87→## Function Prologue
    88→
    89→Standard prologue for non-leaf functions:
    90→
    91→```asm
    92→_func:
    93→        push    fp              ; save caller's frame pointer
    94→        push    r2              ; save register variable
    95→        push    r1              ; save return address
    96→        mov     fp,sp           ; establish new frame
    97→        sub     sp,N            ; allocate N bytes for locals (if needed)
    98→```
    99→
   100→If N is small enough (1..127), use `add sp,-N` instead of `sub sp,N`.
   101→`sub sp,dddddd` is a 4-byte instruction for larger allocations.
   102→
   103→If N is 0 (no locals), the `sub sp` / `add sp` can be omitted --
   104→`mov sp,fp` in the epilogue handles restoration.
   105→
   106→## Function Epilogue
   107→
   108→```asm
   109→        mov     sp,fp           ; deallocate locals
   110→        pop     r1              ; restore return address
   111→        pop     r2              ; restore register variable
   112→        pop     fp              ; restore caller's frame pointer
   113→        jmp     (r1)            ; return to caller
   114→```
   115→
   116→## Stack Frame Layout
   117→
   118→```
   119→Higher addresses (toward initial SP)
   120→+---------------------+
   121→| Argument N          |  fp + 9 + (N-1)*3
   122→| ...                 |
   123→| Argument 2          |  fp + 12
   124→| Argument 1          |  fp + 9
   125→+---------------------+
   126→| Saved r1 (ret addr) |  fp + 6
   127→| Saved r2            |  fp + 3
   128→| Saved fp            |  fp + 0  <-- fp points here
   129→+---------------------+
   130→| Local variable 1    |  fp - 3
   131→| Local variable 2    |  fp - 6
   132→| ...                 |
   133→| Local variable M    |  fp - M*3
   134→+---------------------+  <-- sp (after sub sp,M*3)
   135→Lower addresses (stack grows down)
   136→```
   137→
   138→## Leaf Function Optimization
   139→
   140→If a function does not call any other function (leaf routine), it may
   141→skip saving registers it does not clobber:
   142→
   143→- If r1 is not overwritten, skip `push r1` / `pop r1`.
   144→- If r2 is not used, skip `push r2` / `pop r2`.
   145→- If fp is not needed (no locals accessed via fp), skip frame setup.
   146→
   147→MakerLisp recommends limiting these optimizations to leaf routines only,
   148→to avoid complexity in analyzing callee register usage.
   149→
   150→## Stack Growth and Alignment
   151→
   152→- Stack grows **downward** (push decrements sp by 3).
   153→- Each stack slot is 3 bytes (one 24-bit word).
   154→- sp should remain 3-byte aligned at all times.
   155→- Initial sp is 0xFEEC00 (set by CPU reset).
   156→
   157→## Function Call Sequence Summary
   158→
   159→### Caller Responsibilities
   160→
   161→1. Evaluate arguments.
   162→2. Push arguments right-to-left onto stack.
   163→3. Load target function address: `la r0,_func`
   164→4. Call: `jal r1,(r0)`
   165→5. Clean up arguments: `add sp,N*3`
   166→6. Result is in r0.
   167→
   168→### Callee Responsibilities
   169→
   170→1. Execute prologue (push fp, r2, r1; set fp).
   171→2. Allocate locals if needed.
   172→3. Access arguments at positive fp offsets.
   173→4. Place return value in r0.
   174→5. Execute epilogue (restore sp, pop r1, r2, fp; jmp (r1)).
   175→
   176→## Tail Calls
   177→
   178→A tail call can skip the epilogue and use `jmp` instead of `jal`:
   179→
   180→```asm
   181→        ; instead of: la r0,_other; jal r1,(r0)
   182→        ; followed by epilogue, use:
   183→        mov     sp,fp
   184→        pop     r1
   185→        pop     r2
   186→        pop     fp
   187→        la      r0,_other
   188→        jmp     (r0)            ; tail call (r1 still has original return addr)
   189→```
   190→
   191→## Interrupt Service Routine ABI
   192→
   193→ISRs must save and restore ALL registers they use, including the
   194→condition flag:
   195→
   196→```asm
   197→isr:
   198→        push    r0
   199→        push    r1
   200→        push    r2
   201→        mov     r2,c            ; save condition flag in r2
   202→        push    r2
   203→
   204→        ; ... ISR body ...
   205→
   206→        pop     r2
   207→        clu     z,r2            ; restore condition flag
   208→        pop     r2
   209→        pop     r1
   210→        pop     r0
   211→        jmp     (ir)            ; return from interrupt (clears intis)
   212→```
   213→
   214→## Source
   215→
   216→This ABI is based on:
   217→- Direct email from MakerLisp (COR24 designer), March 2026
   218→- MakerLisp C compiler output (fib.s, sieve.s from COR24-TB demo programs)
   219→- The Portable C Compiler (pcc) calling conventions that MakerLisp adapted
   220→

1→# tc24r Architecture
     2→
     3→## Component Layout
     4→
     5→The compiler is split into 4 components, each a Cargo workspace with
     6→multiple crates under a `crates/` directory:
     7→
     8→```
     9→components/
    10→  core/                          -- shared types
    11→    crates/
    12→      tc24r-ast/                  -- AST node types (Program, Function, Stmt, Expr, Type)
    13→      tc24r-error/                -- CompileError type
    14→      tc24r-span/                 -- Source location (file, line, column)
    15→      tc24r-token/                -- TokenKind enum and Token struct
    16→
    17→  frontend/                      -- source text to AST
    18→    crates/
    19→      tc24r-preprocess/           -- #define, #include, #pragma once
    20→      tc24r-preprocess-tests/     -- preprocessor test suite
    21→      tc24r-lexer/                -- tokenizer (operators, readers, main loop)
    22→      tc24r-lexer-tests/          -- lexer test suite
    23→      tc24r-parse-stream/         -- token stream with expect/peek helpers
    24→      tc24r-parser/               -- recursive-descent parser
    25→      tc24r-parser-tests/         -- parser test suite
    26→
    27→  backend/                       -- AST to assembly
    28→    crates/
    29→      tc24r-codegen/              -- COR24 assembly emitter
    30→      tc24r-codegen-tests/        -- golden file tests and codegen checks
    31→      tc24r-codegen-validate/     -- assembler validation (cor24-run and as24)
    32→
    33→  cli/                           -- binary entry point
    34→    crates/
    35→      tc24r/                      -- main.rs, argument parsing, orchestration
    36→```
    37→
    38→14 crates total. Test crates are separated from library crates so that
    39→test dependencies do not affect the library build.
    40→
    41→## Data Flow
    42→
    43→Source code flows through the compiler in a linear pipeline:
    44→
    45→```
    46→input.c
    47→  |
    48→  v
    49→[preprocess] -- #define substitution, #include expansion, #pragma once
    50→  |
    51→  v
    52→preprocessed source text
    53→  |
    54→  v
    55→[lexer] -- tokenize into TokenKind stream
    56→  |
    57→  v
    58→token stream
    59→  |
    60→  v
    61→[parser] -- recursive descent, produces AST
    62→  |
    63→  v
    64→AST (Program -> Vec<Function> + Vec<Global>)
    65→  |
    66→  v
    67→[codegen] -- walk AST, emit COR24 assembly text
    68→  |
    69→  v
    70→output.s
    71→```
    72→
    73→There is no intermediate representation (IR). The AST is walked directly
    74→by the code generator to emit assembly, following the chibicc approach.
    75→
    76→## Crate Dependencies
    77→
    78→Dependencies flow in one direction: cli -> backend -> frontend -> core.
    79→
    80→```
    81→tc24r (cli)
    82→  |
    83→  +-- tc24r-codegen
    84→  |     +-- tc24r-ast
    85→  |     +-- tc24r-error
    86→  |     +-- tc24r-span
    87→  |
    88→  +-- tc24r-parser
    89→  |     +-- tc24r-ast
    90→  |     +-- tc24r-token
    91→  |     +-- tc24r-parse-stream
    92→  |           +-- tc24r-token
    93→  |           +-- tc24r-span
    94→  |           +-- tc24r-error
    95→  |
    96→  +-- tc24r-lexer
    97→  |     +-- tc24r-token
    98→  |     +-- tc24r-span
    99→  |
   100→  +-- tc24r-preprocess
   101→        +-- tc24r-span
   102→```
   103→
   104→## Design Constraints
   105→
   106→The codebase follows size constraints to keep modules focused:
   107→
   108→- **< 5 public functions per module** -- each .rs file has a narrow API
   109→- **< 5 modules per crate** -- crates stay small and single-purpose
   110→- **Prefer many small crates** over few large ones
   111→- **Test crates are separate** -- tc24r-lexer-tests, tc24r-parser-tests, etc.
   112→- **No circular dependencies** -- strictly layered
   113→
   114→## Key Design Decisions
   115→
   116→### No IR
   117→
   118→The compiler emits assembly directly from the AST. This keeps the codebase
   119→small and follows chibicc's architecture. The tradeoff is that optimization
   120→passes would need to work on either the AST or the assembly text.
   121→
   122→### Push/Pop Expression Evaluation
   123→
   124→Binary expressions are evaluated by:
   125→1. Evaluate left-hand side into r0
   126→2. Push r0
   127→3. Evaluate right-hand side into r0
   128→4. Pop r1 (left-hand side)
   129→5. Apply operator: r0 = r1 op r0
   130→
   131→This is simple and correct but generates more push/pop pairs than necessary.
   132→A future peephole pass could eliminate redundant stack traffic.
   133→
   134→### Stack-Allocated Locals
   135→
   136→At function entry, a pre-pass counts all local variable declarations
   137→(including arrays) and allocates stack space with a single `sub sp,N`.
   138→Variables are accessed at negative offsets from fp. Array elements are
   139→contiguous starting from the variable's base offset.
   140→
   141→### Label Generation
   142→
   143→Labels are generated from a monotonic counter: L0, L1, L2, etc. Each
   144→control-flow construct consumes one or more labels. The counter is global
   145→across all functions in a translation unit.
   146→
   147→### Software Division
   148→
   149→COR24 has no hardware divide instruction. When the compiler encounters
   150→`/` or `%`, it emits calls to `__tc24r_div` or `__tc24r_mod` helper
   151→functions. These are emitted once at the end of the assembly output
   152→if used.
   153→
   154→## Module Breakdown
   155→
   156→### tc24r-codegen Modules
   157→
   158→| Module | Purpose |
   159→|--------|---------|
   160→| lib.rs | Top-level codegen entry, _start, globals, runtime helpers |
   161→| func.rs | Function prologue/epilogue, ISR variant |
   162→| stmt.rs | Statement dispatch (if, while, for, return, decl, assign) |
   163→| expr.rs | Expression dispatch (literals, variables, unary, call, cast) |
   164→| binop.rs | Binary operator code generation |
   165→| emit.rs | Assembly text emission helpers |
   166→| runtime.rs | __tc24r_div and __tc24r_mod implementations |
   167→
   168→### tc24r-parser Modules
   169→
   170→| Module | Purpose |
   171→|--------|---------|
   172→| lib.rs | Top-level parse entry, function and global parsing |
   173→| decl.rs | Type parsing, variable declarations |
   174→| stmt.rs | Statement parsing |
   175→| expr.rs | Expression parsing, precedence climbing |
   176→| arithmetic.rs | Arithmetic and comparison subexpressions |
   177→| bitwise.rs | Bitwise operator subexpressions |
   178→| control.rs | if, while, for, do...while parsing |
   179→
   180→### tc24r-lexer Modules
   181→
   182→| Module | Purpose |
   183→|--------|---------|
   184→| lib.rs | Main tokenizer loop |
   185→| operators.rs | Multi-character operator recognition |
   186→| readers.rs | String, number, identifier readers |
   187→

1→# MakerLisp COR24 — Assembly Emulator
     2→
     3→A browser-based educational emulator for the
     4→[MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) COR24 (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit)
     5→architecture. Written in Rust and compiled to WebAssembly.
     6→
     7→**[Live Demo](https://sw-embed.github.io/cor24-rs/)**
     8→
     9→### Assembler Tab
    10→![COR24 Assembler Tab](images/assembler-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    11→
    12→### C Tab
    13→![COR24 C Pipeline Tab](images/c-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    14→
    15→### Rust Tab
    16→![COR24 Rust Pipeline Tab](images/rust-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    17→
    18→### CLI Pipeline Demos
    19→
    20→![Assembler UART Hello](docs/vhs/asm-uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)
    21→
    22→![Rust Pipeline UART Hello](docs/vhs/uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)
    23→
    24→## Features
    25→
    26→- **Interactive Assembly Editor** — Write and edit COR24 assembly code
    27→- **Step-by-Step Execution** — Debug your code instruction by instruction
    28→- **Multi-Region Memory Viewer** — Program, Stack, and I/O regions with change heatmaps
    29→- **CLI Debugger** (`cor24-dbg`) — GDB-like command-line debugger with breakpoints, UART, and LED/button I/O
    30→- **LGO File Loader** — Load programs assembled with the reference `as24` toolchain
    31→- **Built-in Examples** — Learn from pre-loaded example programs
    32→- **Challenges** — Test your assembly skills with programming challenges
    33→- **ISA Reference** — Instruction set documentation and memory map
    34→
    35→## COR24 Architecture
    36→
    37→COR24 is a 24-bit RISC soft CPU for Lattice MachXO FPGAs, designed for
    38→embedded systems education. 32 operations encode into 211 instruction
    39→forms (1, 2, or 4 bytes).
    40→
    41→- **3 General-Purpose Registers**: r0, r1, r2 (24-bit)
    42→- **5 Special-Purpose Registers**:
    43→  - r3 = fp (frame pointer)
    44→  - r4 = sp (stack pointer, init 0xFEEC00)
    45→  - r5 = z (always zero; usable only in compare instructions)
    46→  - r6 = iv (interrupt vector)
    47→  - r7 = ir (interrupt return address)
    48→- **Single Condition Flag**: C (set by compare instructions)
    49→- **16 MB Address Space**: 1 MB SRAM + 3 KB EBR (stack) + memory-mapped I/O
    50→- **Variable-Length Instructions**: 1, 2, or 4 bytes
    51→
    52→### Supported Instructions
    53→
    54→| Category | Instructions |
    55→|----------|-------------|
    56→| Arithmetic | `add`, `sub`, `mul` |
    57→| Logic | `and`, `or`, `xor` |
    58→| Shifts | `shl`, `sra`, `srl` |
    59→| Compare | `ceq`, `cls`, `clu` |
    60→| Branch | `bra`, `brf`, `brt` |
    61→| Jump | `jmp`, `jal` |
    62→| Load | `la`, `lc`, `lcu`, `lb`, `lbu`, `lw` |
    63→| Store | `sb`, `sw` |
    64→| Stack | `push`, `pop` |
    65→| Move | `mov`, `sxt`, `zxt` |
    66→
    67→## Examples & Demos
    68→
    69→This project has two sets of examples, matching the two tabs in the web UI:
    70→
    71→- **[Assembler Examples](docs/assembler-examples.md)** — 11 hand-written COR24 assembly programs.
    72→  Available in the web UI's **Assembler** tab (click Examples → pick one → Assemble → Run)
    73→  and via `cor24-dbg` on the command line (see `scripts/demo-cli-*.sh`).
    74→
    75→- **[Rust Pipeline Demos](docs/rust-pipeline-demos.md)** — 12 Rust programs compiled through the
    76→  Rust → MSP430 → COR24 cross-compilation pipeline.
    77→  Available in the web UI's **Rust** tab (pick example → Compile → Translate → Assemble → Run)
    78→  and via CLI scripts in `rust-to-cor24/demos/` (see `run-demo.sh`, per-demo `run.sh`, `generate-all.sh`).
    79→
    80→```bash
    81→# Assembler example via CLI debugger
    82→scripts/demo-cli-hello-world.sh
    83→
    84→# Rust pipeline demo via CLI
    85→rust-to-cor24/demos/run-demo.sh demo_add
    86→rust-to-cor24/demos/run-demo.sh demo_echo_v2 --uart-input 'hello!'
    87→```
    88→
    89→For an overview of all the binaries and how they fit together, see **[docs/eli5.md](docs/eli5.md)**.
    90→
    91→## Building
