1→# COR24 ISA Summary
     2→
     3→C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit Instruction Set Architecture.
     4→Designed by MakerLisp for efficient C code execution on FPGA.
     5→
     6→## Registers
     7→
     8→8 registers, all 24-bit wide, plus PC and a 1-bit condition flag.
     9→
    10→| Index | Name | Role | Notes |
    11→|-------|------|------|-------|
    12→| r0 | r0 | General purpose / return value | Not saved across calls; scratch |
    13→| r1 | r1 | Link register (return address) | `jal` always saves return addr here |
    14→| r2 | r2 | General purpose / register variable | Callee-saved |
    15→| r3 | fp | Frame pointer | Callee-saved |
    16→| r4 | sp | Stack pointer | Grows downward; init 0xFEEC00 |
    17→| r5 | z | Zero register | Always reads 0; used in compares |
    18→| r6 | iv | Interrupt vector | Holds ISR address |
    19→| r7 | ir | Interrupt return address | CPU sets on interrupt entry |
    20→| - | PC | Program counter | 24-bit |
    21→| - | C | Condition flag | 1-bit; set only by ceq/cls/clu |
    22→
    23→### Register Constraints
    24→
    25→- Only r0, r1, r2 are truly general-purpose for arithmetic/logic.
    26→- fp and sp have dedicated roles but can appear in some instructions.
    27→- r5 (z) is read-only zero; only usable as an operand in compare instructions.
    28→- r6 (iv) and r7 (ir) are for interrupt handling.
    29→- The C flag is not set by arithmetic -- only by compare instructions.
    30→
    31→## Instruction Formats
    32→
    33→All instructions are 1, 2, or 4 bytes. Never 3 bytes.
    34→
    35→| Format | Bytes | Structure | Used By |
    36→|--------|-------|-----------|---------|
    37→| Register-register | 1 | `[opcode+regs]` | add, sub, mul, and, or, xor, shifts, compares, mov, push, pop, jal, jmp, sxt, zxt |
    38→| Register + imm8 | 2 | `[opcode+reg] [imm8]` | add imm, lc, lcu, lb, lbu, lw, sb, sw, bra, brf, brt |
    39→| Register + imm24 | 4 | `[opcode+reg] [low] [mid] [high]` | la, sub sp |
    40→
    41→The first byte encodes both the opcode (5 bits) and register operand(s) (3 bits).
    42→A 256-entry decode ROM maps each first-byte value to (opcode, ra, rb).
    43→
    44→## Complete Instruction Set (32 Instructions)
    45→
    46→### Arithmetic
    47→
    48→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation | Notes |
    49→|----------|-------|-----------|-------|
    50→| `add ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra + rb | Wraps at 2^24 |
    51→| `add ra,dd` | 2 | ra = ra + sign_ext(dd) | dd is 8-bit signed (-128..127) |
    52→| `sub ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra - rb | Modulo 2^24 |
    53→| `sub sp,dddddd` | 4 | sp = sp - imm24 | Stack allocation; 24-bit unsigned |
    54→| `mul ra,rb` | 1 | ra = (ra * rb)[23:0] | 24-cycle hardware multiply; low 24 bits |
    55→
    56→### Logical
    57→
    58→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation |
    59→|----------|-------|-----------|
    60→| `and ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra & rb |
    61→| `or ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra \| rb |
    62→| `xor ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra ^ rb |
    63→
    64→### Shift
    65→
    66→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation | Notes |
    67→|----------|-------|-----------|-------|
    68→| `shl ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra << rb[4:0] | Left shift |
    69→| `srl ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] | Logical right (zero fill) |
    70→| `sra ra,rb` | 1 | ra = ra >>> rb[4:0] | Arithmetic right (sign fill) |
    71→
    72→Shift amount uses only the low 5 bits of rb.
    73→
    74→### Compare (Set Condition Flag)
    75→
    76→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation |
    77→|----------|-------|-----------|
    78→| `ceq ra,rb` | 1 | C = (ra == rb) |
    79→| `cls ra,rb` | 1 | C = (ra < rb) signed |
    80→| `clu ra,rb` | 1 | C = (ra < rb) unsigned |
    81→
    82→To test if a register is zero: `ceq r0,z` (compare with r5).
    83→To test if negative (signed): `cls r0,z` (less than zero).
    84→
    85→### Branch (PC-Relative)
    86→
    87→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation |
    88→|----------|-------|-----------|
    89→| `bra dd` | 2 | PC = PC + sign_ext(dd) -- unconditional |
    90→| `brf dd` | 2 | if (!C) PC = PC + sign_ext(dd) -- branch if false |
    91→| `brt dd` | 2 | if (C) PC = PC + sign_ext(dd) -- branch if true |
    92→
    93→Branch offset dd is 8-bit signed (-128..+127 bytes).
    94→PC is 2 bytes ahead at execution (past the branch instruction itself).
    95→
    96→### Jump / Call
    97→
    98→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation | Notes |
    99→|----------|-------|-----------|-------|
   100→| `jal ra,(rb)` | 1 | r1 = PC+1; PC = rb | Return addr always saved to r1 |
   101→| `jmp (ra)` | 1 | PC = ra | If ra=r7, clears interrupt flag |
   102→
   103→Three `jal` encodings exist: target in (r0), (r1), or (r2).
   104→The return address is always stored in r1 regardless of encoding.
   105→
   106→### Load
   107→
   108→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation | Notes |
   109→|----------|-------|-----------|-------|
   110→| `la ra,dddddd` | 4 | ra = imm24 | If ra=r7, acts as direct jump |
   111→| `lc ra,dd` | 2 | ra = sign_ext(dd) | 8-bit signed constant |
   112→| `lcu ra,dd` | 2 | ra = zero_ext(dd) | 8-bit unsigned constant |
   113→| `lb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | ra = sign_ext(mem[rb+sign_ext(dd)]) | Load byte, sign-extend |
   114→| `lbu ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | ra = zero_ext(mem[rb+sign_ext(dd)]) | Load byte, zero-extend |
   115→| `lw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | ra = mem24[rb+sign_ext(dd)] | Load 24-bit word |
   116→
   117→### Store
   118→
   119→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation |
   120→|----------|-------|-----------|
   121→| `sb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | mem[rb+sign_ext(dd)] = ra[7:0] |
   122→| `sw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | mem24[rb+sign_ext(dd)] = ra |
   123→
   124→### Move / Extension
   125→
   126→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation | Notes |
   127→|----------|-------|-----------|-------|
   128→| `mov ra,rb` | 1 | ra = rb | If rb=r5(z), loads C flag: ra={0..0,C} |
   129→| `sxt ra,rb` | 1 | ra = sign_ext(rb[7:0]) | Sign-extend byte to 24 bits |
   130→| `zxt ra,rb` | 1 | ra = zero_ext(rb[7:0]) | Zero-extend byte to 24 bits |
   131→
   132→### Stack
   133→
   134→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Operation |
   135→|----------|-------|-----------|
   136→| `push ra` | 1 | sp -= 3; mem24[sp] = ra |
   137→| `pop ra` | 1 | ra = mem24[sp]; sp += 3 |
   138→
   139→Stack operations always move 3 bytes (one 24-bit word).
   140→
   141→## Addressing Modes
   142→
   143→| Mode | Syntax | Description |
   144→|------|--------|-------------|
   145→| Register direct | `add r0,r1` | Operands in registers |
   146→| Immediate 8-bit | `lc r0,42` | 8-bit signed/unsigned constant |
   147→| Immediate 24-bit | `la r0,0x1234` | Full 24-bit constant/address |
   148→| Base + offset | `lw r0,6(fp)` | Base register + 8-bit signed offset |
   149→| Register indirect | `jmp (r1)` | Jump to address in register |
   150→
   151→Base registers for load/store: r0, r1, r2, fp.
   152→sp cannot be used as a base register in lb/lbu/lw/sb/sw -- use fp instead.
   153→
   154→## Immediate Value Ranges
   155→
   156→| Context | Size | Signed Range | Unsigned Range |
   157→|---------|------|-------------|----------------|
   158→| lc, add imm, branch offset, load/store offset | 8-bit | -128..127 | 0..255 (lcu only) |
   159→| la, sub sp | 24-bit | -8388608..8388607 | 0..16777215 |
   160→
   161→## Key Semantic Details
   162→
   163→### Overflow
   164→Silent wraparound at 24 bits (modulo 2^24). No overflow flag.
   165→
   166→### Condition Flag
   167→- Set only by ceq, cls, clu.
   168→- All other instructions preserve C.
   169→- `mov ra,c` (where c means the condition flag via r5 encoding) loads 0 or 1 into ra.
   170→
   171→### Endianness
   172→Little-endian. Multi-byte values stored LSB first.
   173→4-byte instruction `la r0,0x123456` encodes as: `[opcode] [0x56] [0x34] [0x12]`.
   174→
   175→### Multiply
   176→Hardware multiply via `mul ra,rb`. Takes 24 clock cycles.
   177→Returns low 24 bits of the full product. No high-word access.
   178→
   179→### Divide
   180→No hardware divide instruction. Must be implemented in software
   181→via repeated subtraction or lookup table.
   182→
   183→### NOP
   184→No dedicated NOP instruction. Common idioms:
   185→- `zxt z,z` (opcode 0xFF) -- harmless no-op
   186→- Careful: `add r0,r0` doubles r0, not a true NOP
   187→
   188→## Interrupts
   189→
   190→- Interrupt vector address stored in r6 (iv).
   191→- On interrupt: CPU executes `jal r7,(r6)` automatically.
   192→  - Return address saved in r7 (ir).
   193→  - Jumps to address in r6.
   194→- Return from ISR: `jmp (r7)` -- also clears the interrupt-in-service flag.
   195→- Currently only one interrupt source: UART RX data ready.
   196→- Interrupts are detected on control flow transfers (bra, brf, brt, jmp, jal).
   197→
   198→## References
   199→
   200→- Hardware Verilog: `cor24_cpu.v` in COR24-TB archive
   201→- Decode ROM: `dis_rom_init.mem` (256-entry lookup)
   202→- ISA manual: `COR24-TB-MAN.pdf`
   203→- MakerLisp: https://makerlisp.com
   204→- License: MIT
   205→

1→# COR24 Memory Map
     2→
     3→## Address Space
     4→
     5→24-bit address space (16 MB total). Byte-addressable. Little-endian.
     6→
     7→```
     8→0x000000 +---------------------------+
     9→         |                           |
    10→         |  External SRAM            |
    11→         |  (up to 16 MB)            |
    12→         |  512 KB on testboard      |
    13→         |                           |
    14→0xFE0000 +---------------------------+
    15→         |  Embedded Block RAM       |
    16→         |  (4 KB populated)         |
    17→         |                           |
    18→0xFEE000 |  Boot ROM / Program Start | <-- Reset vector (PC starts here)
    19→         |                           |
    20→0xFEEC00 |  Initial Stack Pointer    | <-- SP starts here, grows DOWN
    21→         |                           |
    22→0xFF0000 +---------------------------+
    23→         |  Memory-Mapped I/O        |
    24→         |  (64 KB region)           |
    25→0xFFFFFF +---------------------------+
    26→```
    27→
    28→## Memory Regions
    29→
    30→### External SRAM (0x000000 - 0xFDFFFF)
    31→
    32→- Up to 16 MB addressable.
    33→- COR24-TB testboard has 512 KB populated.
    34→- 2-cycle access per byte; word access takes 6+ cycles.
    35→- Used for: heap, large arrays, program code after relocation.
    36→
    37→### Embedded Block RAM (0xFE0000 - 0xFEFFFF)
    38→
    39→- 4 KB populated on testboard (0xFEE000 - 0xFEFFFF).
    40→- Faster than SRAM (2 cycles from PC load to first byte).
    41→- Used for: boot code, small programs, stack.
    42→- Dual-port: can be read by instruction fetch and data access.
    43→
    44→### Boot / Program Entry (0xFEE000)
    45→
    46→- CPU begins execution here on reset.
    47→- The monitor program (`loadngo`) typically resides here.
    48→- `loadngo` can load user programs into SRAM and jump to them.
    49→
    50→### Stack (Initial SP = 0xFEEC00)
    51→
    52→- Stack pointer initialized to 0xFEEC00 on CPU reset.
    53→- Grows **downward** (push decrements SP by 3).
    54→- Stack is in embedded block RAM for speed.
    55→- Stack size depends on available EBR below 0xFEEC00.
    56→
    57→### Memory-Mapped I/O (0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF)
    58→
    59→See I/O Ports section below.
    60→
    61→## I/O Port Map
    62→
    63→| Address | Read | Write | Description |
    64→|---------|------|-------|-------------|
    65→| 0xFF0000 | Button state (bit 0) | LED state (bit 0) | GPIO |
    66→| 0xFF0010 | Interrupt enable (bit 0) | Set interrupt enable | Interrupt control |
    67→| 0xFF0100 | UART RX data (8-bit) | UART TX data (8-bit) | Serial data |
    68→| 0xFF0101 | UART status | - | Serial status |
    69→
    70→### UART Status Register (0xFF0101) -- Read Only
    71→
    72→| Bit | Name | Meaning |
    73→|-----|------|---------|
    74→| 7 | TX busy | 1 = transmitter is sending a byte |
    75→| 2 | RX overflow | 1 = receive buffer overflowed |
    76→| 1 | CTS | 1 = clear to send (flow control) |
    77→| 0 | RX ready | 1 = received byte available |
    78→
    79→### UART Usage Pattern
    80→
    81→**Transmit a byte:**
    82→```asm
    83→        la      r1,0xFF0100     ; UART base
    84→.wait:  lb      r2,1(r1)        ; read status (bit 7 = TX busy)
    85→        cls     r2,z            ; negative = bit 7 set = busy
    86→        brt     .wait           ; spin while busy
    87→        sb      r0,0(r1)        ; transmit byte from r0[7:0]
    88→```
    89→
    90→**Receive a byte:**
    91→```asm
    92→        la      r1,0xFF0100     ; UART base
    93→        lb      r0,0(r1)        ; read RX data (auto-acknowledges interrupt)
    94→```
    95→
    96→### Interrupt Enable (0xFF0010)
    97→
    98→- Write bit 0 = 1 to enable UART RX interrupt.
    99→- Write bit 0 = 0 to disable.
   100→- Read returns current enable state.
   101→- Only one interrupt source exists (UART RX ready).
   102→
   103→## Memory Model for C Programs
   104→
   105→### Freestanding (Bare Metal)
   106→
   107→Programs run without an OS. The compiler generates:
   108→
   109→- `.text` section: code, placed at program start address
   110→- `.data` section: initialized global/static data
   111→- `.comm` symbols: uninitialized (BSS) global/static data
   112→
   113→### Typical Program Memory Layout
   114→
   115→```
   116→0xFEE000 +---------------------------+
   117→         |  .text (code)             |
   118→         |  Functions, string lits   |
   119→         +---------------------------+
   120→         |  .data (initialized)      |
   121→         |  Global variables         |
   122→         +---------------------------+
   123→         |  .comm / .bss             |
   124→         |  Uninitialized globals    |
   125→         +---------------------------+
   126→         |  (free space)             |
   127→         +---------------------------+
   128→         |  Stack (grows down)       |
   129→0xFEEC00 +---------------------------+ <-- Initial SP
   130→```
   131→
   132→For larger programs loaded via the monitor into SRAM:
   133→
   134→```
   135→0x000000 +---------------------------+
   136→         |  .text (code)             |
   137→         +---------------------------+
   138→         |  .data                    |
   139→         +---------------------------+
   140→         |  .bss                     |
   141→         +---------------------------+
   142→         |  (free / heap)            |
   143→         +---------------------------+
   144→         |  Stack (grows down)       |
   145→         |  (SP set by startup code) |
   146→         +---------------------------+
   147→```
   148→
   149→### Stack Allocation
   150→
   151→- Each push/pop moves 3 bytes (one word).
   152→- Local variables allocated by `sub sp,N` or `add sp,-N`.
   153→- Deallocated by `mov sp,fp` (frame pointer restoration).
   154→- No hardware stack overflow detection.
   155→
   156→### Global Variable Placement
   157→
   158→- Initialized globals go in `.data` section.
   159→- Uninitialized globals declared with `.comm name,size`.
   160→- String literals emitted as `.byte` sequences in `.data`.
   161→
   162→## Hardware Notes
   163→
   164→- UART baud rate: 921600 (at 101.6 MHz clock).
   165→- UART format: 8N1.
   166→- UART RX has 4-entry FIFO.
   167→- LED is active-low on hardware (writing 1 turns LED off).
   168→- Reading UART RX data auto-acknowledges the interrupt.
   169→

1→# Known Issues
     2→
     3→## reg-rs Cross-Platform Output Differences
     4→
     5→**Status:** Resolved (2026-03-22)
     6→
     7→The demo run scripts previously used `sed` with GNU extensions (`\s`) that
     8→behaved differently on macOS (BSD sed) vs Linux (GNU sed). This caused reg-rs
     9→`.out` baselines captured on one platform to fail on the other.
    10→
    11→### Fix Applied
    12→
    13→All `sed` usage in `demos/run-demo*.sh` has been replaced with portable
    14→`awk` alternatives:
    15→
    16→- R0 extraction: `awk -F'[()]' '{print $2}' | tr -d ' '`
    17→- Halted status: `awk '{print $2}'`
    18→- LED state: `awk '{print $3}'`
    19→- UART output: `awk -F'UART TX log:' '{print $2}' | tr -d ' "'`
    20→- IntEn value: `awk '{print $3}'`
    21→
    22→### Remaining Consideration
    23→
    24→The `.out` baselines contain absolute paths (e.g., include directories).
    25→When switching between machines, `reg-rs rebase` must be run to update
    26→baselines with the local paths. reg-rs supports a `preprocess` field in
    27→`.rgt` files that could be used to normalize paths before comparison.
    28→

1→# tc24r Language Reference
     2→
     3→Quick reference for the C subset supported by tc24r, targeting the COR24 architecture.
     4→
     5→## Types
     6→
     7→| Type | Size | Description |
     8→|------|------|-------------|
     9→| `int` | 24-bit (3 bytes) | Native machine word, signed |
    10→| `char` | 8-bit (1 byte) | Signed byte |
    11→| `void` | -- | Return type only |
    12→| `int *` | 24-bit | Pointer to int |
    13→| `char *` | 24-bit | Pointer to char |
    14→| `char **` | 24-bit | Pointer to pointer to char |
    15→
    16→Pointers can be nested to arbitrary depth (`int **`, `char ***`, etc.).
    17→
    18→## Literals
    19→
    20→### Integer Literals
    21→
    22→```c
    23→int x = 42;         // decimal
    24→int y = 0xFF;        // hexadecimal (0x or 0X prefix)
    25→int z = 0xFF0100;    // 24-bit hex address
    26→```
    27→
    28→Small constants (-128 to 127) emit `lc r0,N`. Larger constants emit `la r0,N`.
    29→
    30→### String Literals
    31→
    32→```c
    33→char *msg = "hello\n";
    34→```
    35→
    36→String constants are emitted in .data as `.byte` sequences with a NUL terminator.
    37→Supported escape sequences: `\n`, `\t`, `\r`, `\\`, `\"`, `\0`.
    38→
    39→### Character Values
    40→
    41→There is no char literal syntax. Use integer values directly:
    42→
    43→```c
    44→int ch = 72;     // 'H' == 72
    45→```
    46→
    47→## Operators
    48→
    49→All operators follow standard C precedence, from lowest to highest:
    50→
    51→### Assignment
    52→
    53→```c
    54→x = expr;        // simple assignment
    55→a[i] = expr;     // array element assignment
    56→*p = expr;       // pointer dereference assignment
    57→```
    58→
    59→### Logical (short-circuit)
    60→
    61→```c
    62→a && b           // logical AND -- skips b if a is 0
    63→a || b           // logical OR -- skips b if a is nonzero
    64→```
    65→
    66→### Bitwise
    67→
    68→```c
    69→a | b            // bitwise OR
    70→a ^ b            // bitwise XOR
    71→a & b            // bitwise AND
    72→```
    73→
    74→### Comparison
    75→
    76→```c
    77→a == b           // equal
    78→a != b           // not equal
    79→a < b            // less than
    80→a > b            // greater than
    81→a <= b           // less or equal
    82→a >= b           // greater or equal
    83→```
    84→
    85→### Shift
    86→
    87→```c
    88→a << n           // left shift
    89→a >> n           // right shift (arithmetic)
    90→```
    91→
    92→### Arithmetic
    93→
    94→```c
    95→a + b            // addition
    96→a - b            // subtraction
    97→a * b            // multiplication (hardware, 24-cycle)
    98→a / b            // division (software __tc24r_div)
    99→a % b            // modulo (software __tc24r_mod)
   100→```

1→# COR24 ISA Reference
     2→
     3→C-Oriented RISC 24-bit Instruction Set Architecture
     4→
     5→## Overview
     6→
     7→COR24 is a 24-bit RISC architecture designed for efficient C code execution. It features:
     8→
     9→- **24-bit data path** - Registers and addresses are 24 bits wide
    10→- **3 general-purpose registers** - r0, r1, r2
    11→- **5 special-purpose registers** - fp, sp, z, iv, ir
    12→- **Single condition flag** - C (carry/condition)
    13→- **Variable-length instructions** - 1, 2, or 4 bytes
    14→- **16MB address space** - Full 24-bit addressing
    15→- **Little-endian** byte ordering
    16→- **Memory-mapped I/O**
    17→
    18→## Registers
    19→
    20→| Register | Name | Description |
    21→|----------|------|-------------|
    22→| r0 | - | General purpose / return value |
    23→| r1 | - | Return address (link register) |
    24→| r2 | - | General purpose |
    25→| r3 | fp | Frame pointer |
    26→| r4 | sp | Stack pointer |
    27→| r5 | z | Zero (for compare instructions) |
    28→| r6 | - | Interrupt vector |
    29→| r7 | - | Used for absolute jumps |
    30→| PC | - | Program counter (24-bit) |
    31→| C | - | Condition flag (1-bit) |
    32→
    33→## Memory Map
    34→
    35→| Address Range | Description |
    36→|---------------|-------------|
    37→| 0x000000 - 0xFDFFFF | SRAM (external memory) |
    38→| 0xFE0000 - 0xFEFFFF | Embedded block RAM (fast) |
    39→| 0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF | Memory-mapped I/O |
    40→
    41→The CPU starts execution at address 0xFEE000 on reset.
    42→
    43→## Instruction Set Summary
    44→
    45→### Arithmetic Instructions
    46→
    47→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    48→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    49→| `add ra,rb` | 1 | 0x00 | ra = ra + rb |
    50→| `add ra,dd` | 2 | 0x01 | ra = ra + sign_extend(dd) |
    51→| `sub ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1A | ra = ra - rb |
    52→| `sub sp,dddddd` | 4 | 0x1B | sp = sp - dddddd (24-bit) |
    53→| `mul ra,rb` | 1 | 0x12 | ra = ra * rb (lower 24 bits) |
    54→
    55→### Logical Instructions
    56→
    57→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    58→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    59→| `and ra,rb` | 1 | 0x02 | ra = ra & rb |
    60→| `or ra,rb` | 1 | 0x13 | ra = ra \| rb |
    61→| `xor ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1E | ra = ra ^ rb |
    62→
    63→### Shift Instructions
    64→
    65→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    66→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    67→| `shl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x17 | ra = ra << rb[4:0] |
    68→| `sra ra,rb` | 1 | 0x18 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (arithmetic) |
    69→| `srl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x19 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (logical) |
    70→
    71→### Compare Instructions
    72→
    73→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    74→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    75→| `ceq ra,rb` | 1 | 0x06 | C = (ra == rb) |
    76→| `cls ra,rb` | 1 | 0x07 | C = (ra < rb) signed |
    77→| `clu ra,rb` | 1 | 0x08 | C = (ra < rb) unsigned |
    78→
    79→### Branch Instructions
    80→
    81→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    82→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    83→| `bra dd` | 2 | 0x03 | PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
    84→| `brf dd` | 2 | 0x04 | if (!C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
    85→| `brt dd` | 2 | 0x05 | if (C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
    86→
    87→### Jump Instructions
    88→
    89→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    90→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    91→| `jmp (ra)` | 1 | 0x0A | PC = ra |
    92→| `jal ra,(rb)` | 1 | 0x09 | ra = PC + 1; PC = rb |
    93→
    94→### Load Instructions
    95→
    96→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    97→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    98→| `la ra,dddddd` | 4 | 0x0B | ra = dddddd (24-bit address) |
    99→| `lb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0C | ra = sign_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
   100→| `lbu ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0D | ra = zero_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
   101→| `lc ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0E | ra = sign_extend(dd) |
   102→| `lcu ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0F | ra = zero_extend(dd) |
   103→| `lw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x10 | ra = mem24[rb + dd] |
   104→
   105→### Store Instructions
   106→
   107→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
   108→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
   109→| `sb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x16 | mem[rb + dd] = ra[7:0] |
   110→| `sw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x1C | mem24[rb + dd] = ra |
   111→
   112→### Move Instructions
   113→
   114→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
   115→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
   116→| `mov ra,rb` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = rb |
   117→| `mov ra,c` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = C (when rb=5) |
   118→| `sxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1D | ra = sign_extend(rb[7:0]) |
   119→| `zxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1F | ra = zero_extend(rb[7:0]) |
   120→
   121→### Stack Instructions
   122→
   123→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
   124→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
   125→| `push ra` | 1 | 0x15 | sp -= 3; mem24[sp] = ra |
   126→| `pop ra` | 1 | 0x14 | ra = mem24[sp]; sp += 3 |
   127→
   128→## Instruction Encoding
   129→
   130→Instructions are encoded with a lookup ROM that maps 8-bit opcodes to decoded operations. The instruction byte encodes the operation and register operands together.
   131→
   132→### Single-Byte Instructions (Register Operations)
   133→
   134→| Byte | Instruction |
   135→|------|-------------|
   136→| 0x01 | add r0,r1 |
   137→| 0x02 | add r0,r2 |
   138→| 0x1A | cls r0,r2 |
   139→| 0x1B | cls r1,r0 |
   140→| 0x25 | jal r1,(r0) |
   141→| 0x27 | jmp (r1) |
   142→| 0x57 | mov r0,r2 |
   143→| 0x65 | mov fp,sp |
   144→| 0x69 | mov sp,fp |
   145→| 0x7A | pop r1 |
   146→| 0x7B | pop r2 |
   147→| 0x7C | pop fp |
   148→| 0x7D | push r0 |
   149→| 0x7E | push r1 |
   150→| 0x7F | push r2 |
   151→| 0x80 | push fp |
   152→| 0xCE | clu z,r0 |
   153→
   154→### Two-Byte Instructions (Register + Immediate)
   155→
   156→| First Byte | Instruction Format |
   157→|------------|-------------------|
   158→| 0x09 | add r0,imm8 |
   159→| 0x0B | add r2,imm8 |
   160→| 0x0C | add sp,imm8 |
   161→| 0x13 | bra offset8 |
   162→| 0x14 | brf offset8 |
   163→| 0x15 | brt offset8 |
   164→| 0x2C | lb r0,(r0) with offset |
   165→| 0x2E | lb r0,(r2) with offset |
   166→| 0x44 | lc r0,imm8 |
   167→| 0x45 | lc r1,imm8 |
   168→| 0x46 | lc r2,imm8 |
   169→| 0x4D | lw r0,offset(fp) |
   170→| 0x51 | lw r1,offset(fp) |
   171→| 0x55 | lw r2,offset(fp) |
   172→| 0x82 | sb r0,(r2) with offset |
   173→| 0x84 | sb r1,(r0) with offset |
   174→| 0xA6 | sw r0,offset(fp) |
   175→
   176→### Four-Byte Instructions (Register + 24-bit Address)
   177→
   178→| First Byte | Instruction Format |
   179→|------------|-------------------|
   180→| 0x29 | la r0,addr24 |
   181→| 0x2A | la r1,addr24 |
   182→| 0x2B | la r2,addr24 |
   183→
   184→## Calling Convention
   185→
   186→### Function Prologue
   187→```asm
   188→push    fp          ; Save frame pointer
   189→push    r2          ; Save callee-saved register
   190→push    r1          ; Save return address
   191→mov     fp,sp       ; Set up frame pointer
   192→add     sp,-N       ; Allocate stack space
   193→```
   194→
   195→### Function Epilogue
   196→```asm
   197→mov     sp,fp       ; Restore stack pointer
   198→pop     r1          ; Restore return address
   199→pop     r2          ; Restore callee-saved register
   200→pop     fp          ; Restore frame pointer
   201→jmp     (r1)        ; Return
   202→```
   203→
   204→### Argument Passing
   205→- Arguments are pushed right-to-left onto the stack
   206→- Return value is in r0
   207→- Caller cleans up arguments with `add sp,N`
   208→
   209→### Stack Frame Layout
   210→```
   211→Higher addresses
   212→+----------------+
   213→| Argument N     | fp + (N*3 + 6)
   214→| ...            |
   215→| Argument 1     | fp + 9
   216→+----------------+
   217→| Saved r1       | fp + 6
   218→| Saved r2       | fp + 3
   219→| Saved fp       | fp + 0
   220→+----------------+
   221→| Local var 1    | fp - 3
   222→| Local var 2    | fp - 6
   223→| ...            |
   224→+----------------+
   225→Lower addresses (sp)
   226→```
   227→
   228→## Word Size
   229→
   230→- Words are 24 bits (3 bytes)
   231→- Stack operations push/pop 3 bytes at a time
   232→- Word addresses should be 3-byte aligned for efficiency
   233→
   234→## Example: Fibonacci
   235→
   236→```asm
   237→_fib:
   238→        push    fp              ; Save frame pointer
   239→        push    r2              ; Save r2
   240→        push    r1              ; Save return address
   241→        mov     fp,sp           ; Set up frame
   242→        add     sp,-3           ; Local variable space
   243→        lw      r2,9(fp)        ; Load argument n
   244→
   245→        lc      r0,2            ; Load constant 2
   246→        cls     r2,r0           ; Compare n < 2
   247→        brf     L17             ; Branch if false
   248→
   249→        lc      r0,1            ; Return 1
   250→        bra     L16             ; Jump to epilogue
   251→
   252→L17:
   253→        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
   254→        add     r0,-1           ; r0 = n - 1
   255→        push    r0              ; Push argument
   256→        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
   257→        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-1)
   258→        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
   259→        sw      r0,-3(fp)       ; Save result
   260→
   261→        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
   262→        add     r0,-2           ; r0 = n - 2
   263→        push    r0              ; Push argument
   264→        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
   265→        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-2)
   266→        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
   267→        lw      r1,-3(fp)       ; Load fib(n-1)
   268→        add     r0,r1           ; r0 = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
   269→
   270→L16:
   271→        mov     sp,fp           ; Restore stack
   272→        pop     r1              ; Restore return address
   273→        pop     r2              ; Restore r2
   274→        pop     fp              ; Restore frame pointer
   275→        jmp     (r1)            ; Return
   276→```
   277→
   278→## Interrupts
   279→
   280→- Interrupt vector is in r6
   281→- When an interrupt occurs, `jal r7,(r6)` is executed
   282→- Return from interrupt with `jmp (r7)`
   283→- Interrupts can be disabled by the `intis` flag
   284→
   285→## References
   286→
   287→- Verilog source: `cor24_cpu.v`
   288→- Example programs: `demo/*.lst`
   289→- Hardware manual: `doc/COR24-TB-MAN.pdf`
   290→- MakerLisp: https://makerlisp.com
   291→
   292→## License
   293→
   294→The COR24 CPU is open-source under the MIT License.
   295→

1→# Extended Assembler: Compatibility with Reference cor24 Assembler
     2→
     3→## Background
     4→
     5→The COR24 emulator includes a Rust-based assembler that supports all standard
     6→cor24 instructions plus several convenience extensions. The reference cor24
     7→assembler (`as24`, running as `asld24-server` on queenbee:7412) does not
     8→support these extensions.
     9→
    10→We want hand-written `.s` examples in the Web UI and CLI to be **bug-compatible**
    11→with the reference assembler — meaning they assemble correctly with `as24` as
    12→shipped, regardless of whether our extensions are technically correct. If `as24`
    13→doesn't support `la`, our examples must not use `la`, even though it's a real
    14→hardware instruction. The goal is that a student can copy any example from the
    15→Web UI and assemble it with the tools they actually have.
    16→
    17→## Validation Results
    18→
    19→Instruction-by-instruction encoding comparison shows **100% agreement** on all
    20→85 instruction encodings that both assemblers support. The Rust assembler
    21→produces identical bytes for every standard instruction.
    22→
    23→One minor difference: `nop` — ours emits 1 byte (`0x00` = `add r0,r0`),
    24→reference emits 3 bytes (`00 00 00`).
    25→
    26→## Extensions Our Assembler Supports (Reference Does Not)
    27→
    28→### 1. ~~`la` — Load Address~~ RESOLVED
    29→
    30→**Update (2026-03-16):** Testing revealed `la` IS supported by the reference
    31→assembler. The original test failure was due to hex immediates (`la r0,0xFF0000`
    32→fails, but `la r0,-65536` works). The reference assembler supports `la` with
    33→decimal immediates and with labels (including forward references).
    34→
    35→`la` requires NO changes — just convert hex operands to decimal/negative
    36→notation. Examples: `0xFF0000` → `-65536`, `0xFF0100` → `-65280`,
    37→`0x0100` → `256`.
    38→
    39→### 2. Inline Labels (`label: instruction`)
    40→
    41→Our assembler allows `halt: bra halt` on one line. The reference requires
    42→the label on its own line:
    43→
    44→```
    45→; Our syntax (extended):
    46→halt: bra halt
    47→
    48→; Reference syntax:
    49→halt:
    50→        bra halt
    51→```
    52→
    53→**Impact:** ALL 12 assembler examples use inline labels.
    54→
    55→### 3. `#` Comments
    56→
    57→Our assembler accepts both `;` and `#` as comment characters. The reference
    58→only accepts `;`.
    59→
    60→**Impact:** 1 file (`comments.s`).
    61→
    62→### 4. Hex Immediates in Standard Instructions
    63→
    64→`lc r0, 0x3F` works in our assembler. The reference requires decimal: `lc r0, 63`.
    65→Note: hex in `la` operands is moot since `la` itself is unsupported.
    66→
    67→**Impact:** 1 file (`echo.s`) uses hex in `lc`/`lcu` operands.
    68→
    69→### 5. Other Extensions (unused in examples)
    70→
    71→- `sxt`, `zxt` (sign/zero extend) — not used in any example
    72→- `sub rX, imm` on GP registers — not used (only `sub sp, imm` which is standard)
    73→
    74→## File-by-File Audit
    75→
    76→### Assembler Examples (`src/examples/assembler/`)
    77→
    78→Since `la` is supported by the reference assembler (with decimal operands),
    79→the remaining incompatibilities are: inline labels, `#` comments, and hex
    80→immediates.
    81→
    82→| File | Inline labels | `#` comments | Hex immediates |
    83→|---|---|---|---|
    84→| `add.s` | 1 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    85→| `blink_led.s` | 1 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    86→| `button_echo.s` | 1 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    87→| `comments.s` | 1 | 2 | - |
    88→| `countdown.s` | 1 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    89→| `echo.s` | - | - | 10 (5 in `la`, 5 in `lc`/`lcu`) |
    90→| `fibonacci.s` | 2 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    91→| `memory_access.s` | 1 | - | 4 (in `la`) |
    92→| `multiply.s` | 2 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    93→| `nested_calls.s` | 2 | - | - |
    94→| `stack_variables.s` | 1 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    95→| `uart_hello.s` | 2 | - | 1 (in `la`) |
    96→
    97→### Pipeline Examples (`src/examples/rust_pipeline/*.cor24.s`)
    98→
    99→Translator output (Rust→MSP430→COR24). Must also be reference-compatible.
   100→Currently use hex immediates in `la` operands (e.g., `la r0, 0xFF0000`)
   101→which need converting to decimal (`la r0, -65536`). The translator in
   102→`rust-to-cor24/src/msp430.rs` needs to emit decimal instead of hex for
   103→`la` operands. No other incompatibilities — labels are on their own lines
   104→and no `#` comments or inline labels are generated.
   105→
   106→## Proposed Solution
   107→
   108→### Principle: Bug-Compatible Examples
   109→
   110→Examples must assemble with `as24` as shipped. Since `la` IS supported (just
   111→not with hex operands), the fixes are all trivial text changes.
   112→
   113→### Phase 1: Convert All .s Output to as24-Compatible
   114→
   115→**Hand-written examples (12 files)** — mechanical text changes:
   116→
   117→1. **Split inline labels** — `halt: bra halt` → label on its own line
   118→2. **Replace `#` comments with `;`** — only `comments.s`
   119→3. **Convert hex immediates to decimal** — in `la` operands and `lc`/`lcu`
   120→
   121→**Translator output** (`rust-to-cor24/src/msp430.rs`):
   122→
   123→4. **Emit decimal in `la` operands** — change `format!("0x{:06X}", addr)` to
   124→   emit signed decimal (e.g., `-65536` for `0xFF0000`). Then regenerate all
   125→   pipeline demos and web UI examples.
   126→
   127→Hex-to-decimal conversions needed:
   128→- `0xFF0000` → `-65536` (LED I/O register)
   129→- `0xFF0100` → `-65280` (UART data register)
   130→- `0xFF0010` → `-65520` (interrupt enable register)
   131→- `0x0100` → `256`
   132→- `0x0200` → `512`
   133→- `0x3F` → `63`, `0x21` → `33`, `0x61` → `97`, `0x7B` → `123`, `0xDF` → `223`
   134→
   135→No structural changes needed. All examples can remain `.s` files compatible
   136→with both assemblers.
   137→
   138→### Phase 2 (optional): Preprocessor for .sx Files
   139→
   140→If we want to keep hex-immediate syntax as a convenience, a simple text
   141→preprocessor can expand `.sx` → `.s`:
   142→
   143→```
   144→Source (.sx)  →  Preprocessor  →  Standard .s  →  Assembler  →  Binary
   145→```
   146→
   147→**Single-pass macro expansions (no size changes):**
   148→
   149→| Input | Output |
   150→|---|---|
   151→| `halt: bra halt` | `halt:\n        bra halt` |
   152→| `# comment` | `; comment` |
   153→| `lc r0, 0x3F` | `lc r0, 63` |
   154→| `la r0, 0xFF0100` | `la r0, -65280` |
   155→
   156→No instruction size changes, so branch offsets are unaffected.
   157→
   158→### Phase 3 (optional): Assembler `--strict` Flag
   159→
   160→Add a mode that rejects syntax `as24` wouldn't accept:
   161→- Rejects inline labels, `#` comments, hex immediates
   162→- Validates `.s` files are truly as24-compatible
   163→- Use in CI to prevent regressions
   164→
   165→### Additional Findings from Testing
   166→
   167→- Reference assembler does NOT support `.byte` directive
   168→- Reference assembler `nop` = 3 bytes (`00 00 00`), ours = 1 byte (`00`)
   169→- `jal r1,(r2)` syntax matches between both assemblers
   170→- `la` with forward label references works in the reference assembler
   171→

1→//! COR24 instruction definitions
     2→
     3→use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
     4→
     5→/// COR24 instruction opcodes (5-bit, from decoded ROM)
     6→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
     7→#[repr(u8)]
     8→pub enum Opcode {
     9→    AddReg = 0x00,  // add ra,rb
    10→    AddImm = 0x01,  // add ra,dd
    11→    And = 0x02,     // and ra,rb
    12→    Bra = 0x03,     // bra dd
    13→    Brf = 0x04,     // brf dd
    14→    Brt = 0x05,     // brt dd
    15→    Ceq = 0x06,     // ceq ra,rb
    16→    Cls = 0x07,     // cls ra,rb (compare less signed)
    17→    Clu = 0x08,     // clu ra,rb (compare less unsigned)
    18→    Jal = 0x09,     // jal ra,(rb)
    19→    Jmp = 0x0A,     // jmp (ra)
    20→    La = 0x0B,      // la ra,dddddd
    21→    Lb = 0x0C,      // lb ra,dd(rb) (signed)
    22→    Lbu = 0x0D,     // lbu ra,dd(rb) (unsigned)
    23→    Lc = 0x0E,      // lc ra,dd (signed)
    24→    Lcu = 0x0F,     // lcu ra,dd (unsigned)
    25→    Lw = 0x10,      // lw ra,dd(rb)
    26→    Mov = 0x11,     // mov ra,rb or mov ra,c
    27→    Mul = 0x12,     // mul ra,rb
    28→    Or = 0x13,      // or ra,rb
    29→    Pop = 0x14,     // pop ra
    30→    Push = 0x15,    // push ra
    31→    Sb = 0x16,      // sb ra,dd(rb)
    32→    Shl = 0x17,     // shl ra,rb
    33→    Sra = 0x18,     // sra ra,rb
    34→    Srl = 0x19,     // srl ra,rb
    35→    Sub = 0x1A,     // sub ra,rb
    36→    SubSp = 0x1B,   // sub sp,dddddd
    37→    Sw = 0x1C,      // sw ra,dd(rb)
    38→    Sxt = 0x1D,     // sxt ra,rb
    39→    Xor = 0x1E,     // xor ra,rb
    40→    Zxt = 0x1F,     // zxt ra,rb
    41→    Invalid = 0xFF, // Invalid/unknown opcode
    42→}
    43→
    44→impl From<u8> for Opcode {
    45→    fn from(value: u8) -> Self {
    46→        match value {
    47→            0x00 => Opcode::AddReg,
    48→            0x01 => Opcode::AddImm,
    49→            0x02 => Opcode::And,
    50→            0x03 => Opcode::Bra,
    51→            0x04 => Opcode::Brf,
    52→            0x05 => Opcode::Brt,
    53→            0x06 => Opcode::Ceq,
    54→            0x07 => Opcode::Cls,
    55→            0x08 => Opcode::Clu,
    56→            0x09 => Opcode::Jal,
    57→            0x0A => Opcode::Jmp,
    58→            0x0B => Opcode::La,
    59→            0x0C => Opcode::Lb,
    60→            0x0D => Opcode::Lbu,
    61→            0x0E => Opcode::Lc,
    62→            0x0F => Opcode::Lcu,
    63→            0x10 => Opcode::Lw,
    64→            0x11 => Opcode::Mov,
    65→            0x12 => Opcode::Mul,
    66→            0x13 => Opcode::Or,
    67→            0x14 => Opcode::Pop,
    68→            0x15 => Opcode::Push,
    69→            0x16 => Opcode::Sb,
    70→            0x17 => Opcode::Shl,
    71→            0x18 => Opcode::Sra,
    72→            0x19 => Opcode::Srl,
    73→            0x1A => Opcode::Sub,
    74→            0x1B => Opcode::SubSp,
    75→            0x1C => Opcode::Sw,
    76→            0x1D => Opcode::Sxt,
    77→            0x1E => Opcode::Xor,
    78→            0x1F => Opcode::Zxt,
    79→            _ => Opcode::Invalid,
    80→        }
    81→    }
    82→}
    83→
    84→/// Decoded instruction with opcode and register operands
    85→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
    86→pub struct DecodedInstruction {
    87→    pub opcode: Opcode,
    88→    pub ra: u8, // Destination register (0-7)
    89→    pub rb: u8, // Source register (0-7)
    90→}
    91→
    92→impl DecodedInstruction {
    93→    pub fn new(opcode: Opcode, ra: u8, rb: u8) -> Self {
    94→        Self {
    95→            opcode,
    96→            ra: ra & 0x07,
    97→            rb: rb & 0x07,
    98→        }
    99→    }
   100→
   101→    /// Decode from 12-bit ROM output
   102→    pub fn from_decoded(decoded: u16) -> Self {
   103→        let opcode = Opcode::from(((decoded >> 6) & 0x1F) as u8);
   104→        let ra = ((decoded >> 3) & 0x07) as u8;
   105→        let rb = (decoded & 0x07) as u8;
   106→        Self { opcode, ra, rb }
   107→    }
   108→}
   109→
   110→/// Instruction format type
   111→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
   112→pub enum InstructionFormat {
   113→    /// Single byte: opcode + registers encoded
   114→    SingleByte,
   115→    /// Two bytes: first byte + 8-bit immediate/offset
   116→    TwoBytes,
   117→    /// Four bytes: first byte + 24-bit address
   118→    FourBytes,
   119→}
   120→
   121→impl Opcode {
   122→    /// Get the instruction format for this opcode
   123→    pub fn format(&self) -> InstructionFormat {
   124→        match self {
   125→            // Single-byte instructions (register operations only)
   126→            Opcode::AddReg
   127→            | Opcode::And
   128→            | Opcode::Ceq
   129→            | Opcode::Cls
   130→            | Opcode::Clu
   131→            | Opcode::Jal
   132→            | Opcode::Jmp
   133→            | Opcode::Mov
   134→            | Opcode::Mul
   135→            | Opcode::Or
   136→            | Opcode::Pop
   137→            | Opcode::Push
   138→            | Opcode::Shl
   139→            | Opcode::Sra
   140→            | Opcode::Srl
   141→            | Opcode::Sub
   142→            | Opcode::Sxt
   143→            | Opcode::Xor
   144→            | Opcode::Zxt => InstructionFormat::SingleByte,
   145→
   146→            // Two-byte instructions (register + 8-bit immediate)
   147→            Opcode::AddImm
   148→            | Opcode::Bra
   149→            | Opcode::Brf
   150→            | Opcode::Brt
   151→            | Opcode::Lb
   152→            | Opcode::Lbu
   153→            | Opcode::Lc
   154→            | Opcode::Lcu
   155→            | Opcode::Lw
   156→            | Opcode::Sb
   157→            | Opcode::Sw => InstructionFormat::TwoBytes,
   158→
   159→            // Four-byte instructions (register + 24-bit address)
   160→            Opcode::La | Opcode::SubSp => InstructionFormat::FourBytes,
   161→
   162→            Opcode::Invalid => InstructionFormat::SingleByte,
   163→        }
   164→    }
   165→
   166→    /// Get mnemonic string for this opcode
   167→    pub fn mnemonic(&self) -> &'static str {
   168→        match self {
   169→            Opcode::AddReg | Opcode::AddImm => "add",
   170→            Opcode::And => "and",
   171→            Opcode::Bra => "bra",
   172→            Opcode::Brf => "brf",
   173→            Opcode::Brt => "brt",
   174→            Opcode::Ceq => "ceq",
   175→            Opcode::Cls => "cls",
   176→            Opcode::Clu => "clu",
   177→            Opcode::Jal => "jal",
   178→            Opcode::Jmp => "jmp",
   179→            Opcode::La => "la",
   180→            Opcode::Lb => "lb",
   181→            Opcode::Lbu => "lbu",
   182→            Opcode::Lc => "lc",
   183→            Opcode::Lcu => "lcu",
   184→            Opcode::Lw => "lw",
   185→            Opcode::Mov => "mov",
   186→            Opcode::Mul => "mul",
   187→            Opcode::Or => "or",
   188→            Opcode::Pop => "pop",
   189→            Opcode::Push => "push",
   190→            Opcode::Sb => "sb",
   191→            Opcode::Shl => "shl",
   192→            Opcode::Sra => "sra",
   193→            Opcode::Srl => "srl",
   194→            Opcode::Sub | Opcode::SubSp => "sub",
   195→            Opcode::Sw => "sw",
   196→            Opcode::Sxt => "sxt",
   197→            Opcode::Xor => "xor",
   198→            Opcode::Zxt => "zxt",
   199→            Opcode::Invalid => "???",
   200→        }
   201→    }
   202→}
   203→
   204→/// Register names
   205→pub const REG_NAMES: [&str; 8] = ["r0", "r1", "r2", "fp", "sp", "z", "r6", "r7"];
   206→
   207→/// Get register name
   208→pub fn reg_name(reg: u8) -> &'static str {
   209→    REG_NAMES[(reg & 0x07) as usize]
   210→}
   211→

1→//! COR24 CPU state
     2→//!
     3→//! Memory map (MakerLisp COR24):
     4→//!   0x000000 - 0x0FFFFF  SRAM (1 MB on-board)
     5→//!   0x100000 - 0xFDFFFF  Unmapped (addressable, returns 0)
     6→//!   0xFEE000 - 0xFEFFFF  Embedded Block RAM (8 KB window, 3 KB populated)
     7→//!   0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF  I/O space
     8→
     9→use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
    10→use std::fmt;
    11→
    12→use super::instruction::Opcode;
    13→
    14→/// SRAM size: 1 MB on-board
    15→pub const SRAM_SIZE: usize = 0x100000;
    16→
    17→/// EBR (Embedded Block RAM) base address
    18→pub const EBR_BASE: u32 = 0xFEE000;
    19→
    20→/// EBR size: 8 KB window (only 3 KB physically populated on MachXO)
    21→pub const EBR_SIZE: usize = 0x2000;
    22→
    23→/// I/O region base address
    24→pub const IO_BASE: u32 = 0xFF0000;
    25→
    26→/// Default reset address (programs loaded at 0 by monitor)
    27→pub const RESET_ADDRESS: u32 = 0x000000;
    28→
    29→/// Stack pointer initial value (top of EBR populated area)
    30→pub const INITIAL_SP: u32 = 0xFEEC00;
    31→
    32→// Memory-mapped I/O addresses (24-bit)
    33→/// LED/Switch data register: write bit 0 to control LED D2, read bit 0 for button S2
    34→pub const IO_LEDSWDAT: u32 = 0xFF0000;
    35→/// Interrupt enable register: bit 0 = UART RX interrupt enable
    36→pub const IO_INTENABLE: u32 = 0xFF0010;
    37→/// UART data register: write to transmit, read to receive (auto-acknowledges RX)
    38→pub const IO_UARTDATA: u32 = 0xFF0100;
    39→/// UART status register:
    40→///   bit 0: RX data ready
    41→///   bit 1: CTS active
    42→///   bit 2: RX overflow
    43→///   bit 7: TX busy
    44→pub const IO_UARTSTAT: u32 = 0xFF0101;
    45→
    46→/// Kept for backward compatibility but no longer used in I/O dispatch.
    47→/// The MEMORY_SIZE constant is used by app.rs / wasm.rs for the memory viewer.
    48→/// TODO: remove once web UI is updated to use region-based model.
    49→pub const MEMORY_SIZE: usize = SRAM_SIZE;
    50→
    51→/// I/O peripheral state
    52→#[derive(Clone, Default, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    53→pub struct IoState {
    54→    /// LED output state (bit 0 = LED D2, active low on hardware)
    55→    pub leds: u8,
    56→    /// Switch/button input state (bit 0 = button S2, normally high, low when pressed)
    57→    pub switches: u8,
    58→    /// UART transmit buffer (most recent byte sent)
    59→    pub uart_tx: u8,
    60→    /// UART TX busy flag (false = ready to transmit)
    61→    pub uart_tx_busy: bool,
    62→    /// UART TX busy countdown (instructions remaining until TX ready)
    63→    pub uart_tx_countdown: u8,
    64→    /// Number of instructions UART stays busy after a write (default: 10)
    65→    pub uart_tx_busy_cycles: u8,
    66→    /// If true, UART TX busy never clears (for testing poll-before-write)
    67→    pub uart_never_ready: bool,
    68→    /// Count of characters dropped due to writing while TX busy
    69→    pub uart_tx_dropped: u32,
    70→    /// UART receive buffer
    71→    pub uart_rx: u8,
    72→    /// UART receive data ready flag
    73→    pub uart_rx_ready: bool,
    74→    /// UART RX overflow flag
    75→    pub uart_rx_overflow: bool,
    76→    /// Interrupt enable register (bit 0 = UART RX interrupt)
    77→    pub int_enable: u8,
    78→    /// Output text buffer (for UART terminal)
    79→    pub uart_output: String,
    80→}
    81→
    82→impl IoState {
    83→    pub fn new() -> Self {
    84→        Self {
    85→            leds: 0,
    86→            switches: 0x01,          // S2 normally high (low = pressed)
    87→            uart_tx_busy_cycles: 10, // realistic: 10 instructions per character
    88→            uart_never_ready: false,
    89→            uart_tx_dropped: 0,
    90→            uart_tx: 0,
    91→            uart_tx_busy: false, // TX starts ready
    92→            uart_tx_countdown: 0,
    93→            uart_rx: 0,
    94→            uart_rx_ready: false,
    95→            uart_rx_overflow: false,
    96→            int_enable: 0,
    97→            uart_output: String::new(),
    98→        }
    99→    }
   100→}
   101→
   102→/// A single instruction execution record
   103→#[derive(Clone, Debug)]
   104→pub struct TraceEntry {
   105→    /// Program counter at start of instruction
   106→    pub pc: u32,
   107→    /// Decoded opcode
   108→    pub opcode: Opcode,
   109→    /// Destination register index
   110→    pub ra: u8,
   111→    /// Source register index
   112→    pub rb: u8,
   113→    /// Instruction size in bytes (1, 2, or 4)
   114→    pub size: u8,
   115→    /// 8-bit immediate (for 2-byte and 4-byte instructions)
   116→    pub imm8: u8,
   117→    /// 24-bit immediate (for 4-byte instructions)
   118→    pub imm24: u32,
   119→    /// Register state before execution
   120→    pub regs_before: [u32; 8],
   121→    /// Register state after execution
   122→    pub regs_after: [u32; 8],
   123→    /// Condition flag before
   124→    pub c_before: bool,
   125→    /// Condition flag after
   126→    pub c_after: bool,
   127→    /// SP before (convenience, same as regs_before\[4\])
   128→    pub sp_before: u32,
   129→    /// Instruction count at this point
   130→    pub instruction_num: u64,
   131→}
   132→
   133→/// Register names for display
   134→const REG_NAMES: [&str; 8] = ["r0", "r1", "r2", "fp", "sp", "z/c", "iv", "ir"];
   135→
   136→impl fmt::Display for TraceEntry {
   137→    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
   138→        // Disassemble the instruction
   139→        let disasm = self.disassemble();
   140→
   141→        write!(f, "{:5} 0x{:06X}  {:<24}", self.instruction_num, self.pc, disasm)?;
   142→
   143→        // Show register changes
   144→        let mut changes = Vec::new();
   145→        for (i, name) in REG_NAMES.iter().enumerate() {
   146→            if self.regs_before[i] != self.regs_after[i] {
   147→                changes.push(format!("{}:0x{:06X}→0x{:06X}",
   148→                    name, self.regs_before[i], self.regs_after[i]));
   149→            }
   150→        }

1→//! Code generator state for the COR24 compiler.
     2→//!
     3→//! This crate owns the `CodegenState` struct that holds all mutable state
     4→//! used during code generation. Handler crates depend on this crate (and
     5→//! tc24r-traits`) so they can manipulate codegen state without depending
     6→//! tc24r-codegen` crate.
     7→
     8→mod state;
     9→
    10→pub use state::CodegenState;
    11→

1→use std::collections::{HashMap, HashSet};
     2→
     3→use tc24r_ast::Type;
     4→
     5→/// All mutable state carried through code generation.
     6→#[derive(Default)]
     7→pub struct CodegenState {
     8→    /// Accumulated assembly output.
     9→    pub out: String,
    10→    /// Monotonic counter for generating unique labels.
    11→    pub label_counter: usize,
    12→    /// Map from local variable name to its stack-frame offset.
    13→    pub locals: HashMap<String, i32>,
    14→    /// Map from local variable name to its type.
    15→    pub local_types: HashMap<String, Type>,
    16→    /// Total bytes allocated for locals in the current frame.
    17→    pub locals_size: i32,
    18→    /// Set of declared global variable names.
    19→    pub globals: HashSet<String>,
    20→    /// Map from global variable name to its type.
    21→    pub global_types: HashMap<String, Type>,
    22→    /// Label to jump to for a `return` statement.
    23→    pub return_label: String,
    24→    /// String literals collected during generation (emitted in .data).
    25→    pub string_literals: Vec<String>,
    26→    /// Whether the program needs the division runtime helper.
    27→    pub needs_div: bool,
    28→    /// Whether the program needs the modulo runtime helper.
    29→    pub needs_mod: bool,
    30→    /// Stack of break-target labels for loops.
    31→    pub break_labels: Vec<String>,
    32→    /// Stack of continue-target labels for loops.
    33→    pub continue_labels: Vec<String>,
    34→    /// Whether the program needs the printf runtime helpers.
    35→    pub needs_printf: bool,
    36→    /// Struct type registry: tag name → full struct type.
    37→    pub struct_types: HashMap<String, Type>,
    38→    /// Function return type registry: function name → return type.
    39→    pub function_types: HashMap<String, Type>,
    40→}
    41→

     7→use crate::cpu::instruction::Opcode;

     9→/// Encode instruction to byte. Returns None if encoding not found.
    10→/// For single-byte instructions, returns the opcode byte.
    11→/// For multi-byte instructions, returns the first byte (opcode + register info).

    14→    let rb = rb & 0x07;

    18→            (0, 0) => Some(0x00), // r0,r0
    19→            (0, 1) => Some(0x01), // r0,r1
    20→            (0, 2) => Some(0x02), // r0,r2
    21→            (0, 3) => Some(0x58), // r0,fp
    22→            (1, 0) => Some(0x03), // r1,r0
    23→            (1, 1) => Some(0x04), // r1,r1
    24→            (1, 2) => Some(0x05), // r1,r2

    28→            (2, 2) => Some(0x08), // r2,r2

1→; Multiply: 6 x 7 = 42, two ways
     2→; Native mul vs repeated addition loop
     3→; Asserts both methods produce 42
     4→
     5→        ; --- Method 1: native mul instruction ---
     6→        lc      r0,6
     7→        lc      r1,7
     8→        mul     r0,r1           ; r0 = 42
     9→
    10→        ; Assert r0 == 42
    11→        lc      r1,42
    12→        ceq     r0,r1
    13→        brf     assert_fail
    14→
    15→        ; --- Method 2: repeated addition loop ---
    16→        lc      r0,0            ; sum = 0
    17→        lc      r1,7            ; counter = 7
    18→loop:
    19→        add     r0,6            ; sum += 6
    20→        add     r1,-1           ; --counter
    21→        ceq     r1,z
    22→        brf     loop
    23→
    24→        ; Assert r0 == 42
    25→        lc      r1,42
    26→        ceq     r0,r1
    27→        brf     assert_fail
    28→
    29→        ; Both methods agree — print "42 42\n"
    30→        push    r0              ; save result
    31→        la      r2,print2
    32→        jal     r1,(r2)
    33→
    34→        lc      r0,32           ; ' '
    35→        la      r2,putc
    36→        jal     r1,(r2)
    37→
    38→        pop     r0              ; restore result
    39→        la      r2,print2
    40→        jal     r1,(r2)
    41→
    42→        lc      r0,10           ; '\n'
    43→        la      r2,putc
    44→        jal     r1,(r2)
    45→
    46→halt:
    47→        bra     halt
    48→
    49→; Assertion failed — spins here so you can
    50→; inspect registers to see what went wrong
    51→assert_fail:
    52→        bra     assert_fail
    53→
    54→; print2: print r0 as 1-2 digit decimal
    55→print2:
    56→        push    r1
    57→        lc      r1,0
    58→.div10:
    59→        lc      r2,10
    60→        clu     r0,r2
    61→        brt     .ones
    62→        sub     r0,r2
    63→        add     r1,1
    64→        bra     .div10
    65→.ones:
    66→        push    r0
    67→        ceq     r1,z
    68→        brt     .notens
    69→        push    r1
    70→        lc      r0,48
    71→        add     r0,r1
    72→        la      r2,putc
    73→        jal     r1,(r2)
    74→        pop     r1
    75→.notens:
    76→        pop     r0
    77→        lc      r1,48
    78→        add     r0,r1
    79→        la      r2,putc
    80→        jal     r1,(r2)
    81→        pop     r1
    82→        jmp     (r1)

    84→; putc: send byte in r0, polling TX busy

     3→/// An expression node.
     4→#[derive(Debug)]
     5→pub enum Expr {
     6→    IntLit(i32),
     7→    StringLit(String),
     8→    Ident(String),
     9→    Assign {
    10→        name: String,
    11→        value: Box<Expr>,
    12→    },
    13→    Call {
    14→        name: String,
    15→        args: Vec<Expr>,
    16→    },
    17→    BinOp {
    18→        op: BinOp,
    19→        lhs: Box<Expr>,
    20→        rhs: Box<Expr>,
    21→    },
    22→    UnaryOp {
    23→        op: UnaryOp,
    24→        operand: Box<Expr>,
    25→    },
    26→    /// Address-of: &x
    27→    AddrOf(String),
    28→    /// Pointer dereference: *p
    29→    Deref(Box<Expr>),
    30→    /// Cast expression: (type)expr
    31→    Cast {
    32→        ty: crate::Type,
    33→        expr: Box<Expr>,
    34→    },
    35→    /// Dereference assignment: *p = val
    36→    DerefAssign {
    37→        ptr: Box<Expr>,
    38→        value: Box<Expr>,
    39→    },
    40→    /// Pre-increment: ++i (returns new value)
    41→    PreInc(String),
    42→    /// Pre-decrement: --i (returns new value)
    43→    PreDec(String),
    44→    /// Post-increment: i++ (returns old value)
    45→    PostInc(String),
    46→    /// Post-decrement: i-- (returns old value)
    47→    PostDec(String),
    48→    /// Struct member access: expr.member
    49→    MemberAccess {
    50→        object: Box<Expr>,
    51→        member: String,
    52→    },
    53→    /// Struct member assignment: expr.member = value
    54→    MemberAssign {
    55→        object: Box<Expr>,
    56→        member: String,
    57→        value: Box<Expr>,
    58→    },
    59→    /// GCC statement expression: ({ stmt1; stmt2; expr; })
    60→    StmtExpr(crate::Block),
    61→    /// Ternary: cond ? then_expr : else_expr
    62→    Ternary {
    63→        cond: Box<Expr>,
    64→        then_expr: Box<Expr>,
    65→        else_expr: Box<Expr>,
    66→    },
    67→}

1→# COR24 Assembler (as24) Notes
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→The `asld24` toolchain provides a COR24 assembler (`cor24-as`), linker (`cor24-ld`), and a
     6→load-command compactor (`longlgo`). These are C programs built from source in
     7→`docs/research/asld24/`. We do not have access to the COR24 C compiler; our goal is to
     8→generate correct COR24 assembly from Rust.
     9→
    10→## Building the tools
    11→
    12→```bash
    13→cd docs/research/asld24
    14→make
    15→```
    16→
    17→This produces three binaries: `cor24-as`, `cor24-ld`, and `longlgo`.
    18→
    19→## Usage
    20→
    21→The assembler reads from **stdin** (not a filename argument).
    22→
    23→### Flags
    24→
    25→| Flag | Description |
    26→|------|-------------|
    27→| `-l` | Listing output (assemble only, no link) |
    28→| `-S` | Assembly output |
    29→| `-c` | Assemble only (object output) |
    30→| `-O` | Optimize |
    31→| `-a` | Additional option |
    32→
    33→### Assemble with listing (no link)
    34→
    35→```bash
    36→./cor24-as -l < fib.s
    37→./cor24-as -l < sieve.s
    38→```
    39→
    40→This prints addresses, hex opcodes, and the disassembled instructions. Useful for
    41→verifying that our Rust-generated assembly encodes correctly.
    42→
    43→### Assemble and link
    44→
    45→```bash
    46→./cor24-as < sieve.s
    47→```
    48→
    49→This runs the assembler followed by the linker, producing COR24 monitor load/go
    50→commands on stdout. Only works for self-contained programs (no unresolved externals).
    51→
    52→### Assemble, link, and compact
    53→
    54→```bash
    55→./cor24-as < sieve.s | ./longlgo
    56→```
    57→
    58→Consolidates load commands into longer lines for efficient transfer to the monitor.
    59→
    60→## Example files
    61→
    62→### fib.s
    63→
    64→Fibonacci program compiled from C. Computes `fib(33)` and prints the result.
    65→
    66→- **Links?** No -- references `_printf`, which requires a C library we don't have.
    67→- **Assemble only:** `./cor24-as -l < fib.s`
    68→
    69→Key patterns visible in the listing:
    70→
    71→- Function prologue: `push fp` / `push r2` / `push r1` / `mov fp,sp` / `add sp,-N`
    72→- Function epilogue: `mov sp,fp` / `pop r1` / `pop r2` / `pop fp` / `jmp (r1)`
    73→- Function call: `push` args, `la r0,<func>`, `jal r1,(r0)`, `add sp,N`
    74→- Recursive calls to `_fib` with `r2` as the preserved argument
    75→
    76→### sieve.s
    77→
    78→Sieve of Eratosthenes (1000 iterations, primes up to 8190). Compiled from C.
    79→
    80→- **Links?** Yes -- self-contained with built-in I/O (`_putchr` talks directly to UART).
    81→- **Assemble and link:** `./cor24-as < sieve.s | ./longlgo`
    82→- **Assemble only:** `./cor24-as -l < sieve.s`
    83→
    84→Notable details:
    85→
    86→- `_putchr` does direct UART I/O at address `-65280` (0xFF0100), polling status register
    87→- `_printn` does decimal conversion using a `_divdec` table (1000, 100, 10)
    88→- `_putstr` walks a null-terminated string byte by byte
    89→- `.comm _flags,8191` allocates the sieve array in BSS
    90→
    91→## COR24 architecture quick reference
    92→
    93→- **Address space:** 24-bit (16 MB)
    94→- **GP registers:** r0, r1, r2 (only 3 truly general-purpose)
    95→- **Special registers:** fp (r3), sp (r4), z (r5, always zero, compare-only), r6 (iv), r7 (ir)
    96→- **Instruction sizes:** 1, 2, or 4 bytes (never 3)
    97→- **Branch displacement:** PC-relative; PC is 2 ahead of the branch instruction at execute time
    98→- **Stack:** grows downward; `push` decrements sp by 3 (word size), `pop` increments by 3
    99→- **Word size:** 3 bytes (24 bits)
   100→- **UART:** at 0xFF0100 (data) / 0xFF0101 (status)
   101→- **Silent overflow:** wraps at 24 bits
   102→
   103→## Using as24 to validate Rust output
   104→
   105→To test assembly generated by our Rust pipeline:
   106→
   107→1. Write the `.s` file with COR24 assembly syntax
   108→2. Run `./cor24-as -l < yourfile.s` and inspect the listing
   109→3. Compare hex opcodes against expected encodings
   110→4. For self-contained programs, link with `./cor24-as < yourfile.s | ./longlgo` and
   111→   load onto the FPGA via the monitor
   112→

1→# ELI5: What's in This Project?
     2→
     3→This project is a set of tools for working with the COR24 CPU — a small
     4→24-bit processor used for teaching embedded systems. You can write
     5→programs for it, assemble them into machine code, and run them in an
     6→emulator — all without needing the physical hardware.
     7→
     8→There are **two ways** to write programs for the COR24:
     9→
    10→1. **Write assembly by hand** — you write COR24 instructions directly
    11→2. **Write Rust** — a compiler translates your Rust code into COR24 assembly automatically
    12→
    13→## The Binaries
    14→
    15→### Web Emulator (the main app)
    16→
    17→**What:** A website that runs in your browser.
    18→**Built by:** `./build.sh` (or `./serve.sh` for development).
    19→**Output:** `pages/` directory, deployed to GitHub Pages.
    20→
    21→This is the primary way to use the project. It has two tabs:
    22→- **Assembler tab** — paste or write COR24 assembly, click Assemble, click Run
    23→- **Rust tab** — browse pre-built Rust examples showing each compilation stage
    24→
    25→There is no standalone binary for this — it compiles to WebAssembly and
    26→runs in the browser.
    27→
    28→### `cor24-dbg` — CLI Debugger
    29→
    30→**What:** A GDB-like command-line debugger for COR24 programs.
    31→**Where:** `cli/` directory.
    32→**Build:** `cargo build -p cor24-cli`
    33→**Binary:** `target/debug/cor24-dbg`
    34→
    35→**When to use:** When you want to debug a COR24 program from the terminal
    36→instead of the web UI. Supports breakpoints, single-stepping, register
    37→inspection, memory dumps, and UART output capture.
    38→
    39→```bash
    40→# Load a pre-assembled .lgo file and debug it
    41→./target/debug/cor24-dbg tests/programs/hello_world.lgo
    42→
    43→# Commands inside the debugger:
    44→#   run 1000     — run 1000 instructions
    45→#   step         — single step
    46→#   regs         — show registers
    47→#   uart         — show UART output
    48→#   break 0x20   — set breakpoint at address
    49→#   quit         — exit
    50→```
    51→
    52→**Demo scripts:** `scripts/demo-cli-*.sh` build and run various examples
    53→through the debugger.
    54→
    55→### `msp430-to-cor24` — MSP430 → COR24 Translator
    56→
    57→**What:** Translates MSP430 assembly into COR24 assembly.
    58→**Where:** `rust-to-cor24/` directory.
    59→**Build:** `cargo build --release` (in `rust-to-cor24/`)
    60→**Binary:** `target/release/msp430-to-cor24`
    61→
    62→**When to use:** This is the middle step of the Rust pipeline. You don't
    63→usually call it directly — the demo scripts handle it. But if you're
    64→compiling your own Rust code for COR24, you'd use it like:
    65→
    66→```bash
    67→# Rust → MSP430 (done by rustc)
    68→rustup run nightly cargo rustc --target msp430-none-elf \
    69→    -Z build-std=core --release -- --emit asm
    70→
    71→# MSP430 → COR24 (this tool)
    72→msp430-to-cor24 my_program.msp430.s -o my_program.cor24.s
    73→```
    74→
    75→**Why MSP430?** There's no `rustc` backend for COR24, but MSP430 is a
    76→similar 16-bit architecture that Rust can already target. The translator
    77→maps MSP430 registers and instructions to their COR24 equivalents.
    78→
    79→### `cor24-run` — Headless Emulator
    80→
    81→**What:** Assembles a `.cor24.s` file and runs it in the emulator, then
    82→dumps registers, memory, and UART output.
    83→**Where:** `rust-to-cor24/` directory.
    84→**Build:** `cargo build --release` (in `rust-to-cor24/`)
    85→**Binary:** `target/release/cor24-run`
    86→
    87→**When to use:** When you want to run a COR24 assembly program
    88→non-interactively and see the results. Used by the demo scripts.
    89→
    90→```bash
    91→# Assemble and run with register/memory dump
    92→cor24-run --run program.cor24.s --dump --speed 0
    93→
    94→# With a time limit (seconds) and instruction limit
    95→cor24-run --run program.cor24.s --dump --speed 0 --time 5 -n 100000
    96→
    97→# Feed UART input (for interactive programs like echo)
    98→cor24-run --run echo.cor24.s --dump --speed 0 --uart-input 'abc\x21'
    99→```
   100→
   101→### `wasm2cor24` — Experimental WASM → COR24 (unused)
   102→
   103→**What:** An experimental translator from WebAssembly to COR24.
   104→**Where:** `rust-to-cor24/` directory.
   105→**Status:** Early prototype, not used by any demos. The MSP430 path
   106→(`msp430-to-cor24`) is the active pipeline.
   107→
   108→## How They Fit Together
   109→
   110→```
   111→                        ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
   112→  Hand-written          │         Web Emulator (browser)      │
   113→  COR24 assembly ──────►│  Assembler tab: edit → assemble → run│
   114→                        │  Rust tab: browse pre-built examples │
   115→                        └─────────────────────────────────────┘
   116→
   117→  Hand-written          ┌──────────────┐
   118→  COR24 assembly ──────►│  cor24-dbg   │  (CLI debugger)
   119→  (.lgo files)          └──────────────┘
   120→
   121→                        ┌──────────┐    ┌─────────────────┐    ┌──────────┐
   122→  Rust source ─────────►│  rustc   │───►│ msp430-to-cor24 │───►│ cor24-run│
   123→  (.rs)        (MSP430) │(compiler)│    │  (translator)   │    │(emulator)│
   124→                        └──────────┘    └─────────────────┘    └──────────┘
   125→```
   126→
   127→## Quick Start
   128→
   129→```bash
   130→# Run the web emulator locally
   131→./serve.sh
   132→# Open http://localhost:7401/cor24-rs/
   133→
   134→# Run an assembler demo from CLI
   135→scripts/demo-cli-hello-world.sh
   136→
   137→# Run a Rust pipeline demo from CLI
   138→rust-to-cor24/demos/run-demo.sh demo_add --skip-compile
   139→```
   140→
   141→## See Also
   142→
   143→- [Assembler Examples](assembler-examples.md) — hand-written COR24 assembly programs
   144→- [Rust Pipeline Demos](rust-pipeline-demos.md) — Rust programs compiled to COR24
   145→- [rust-to-cor24/README.md](../rust-to-cor24/README.md) — translator internals
   146→

1→//! Startup code emission.
     2→
     3→use tc24r_codegen_state::CodegenState;
     4→use tc24r_emit_macros::emit_lines;
     5→
     6→/// Emit startup code that calls `_main` and halts.
     7→pub fn emit_start(state: &mut CodegenState) {
     8→    emit_lines!(
     9→        state,
    10→        "        .globl  _start",
    11→        "_start:",
    12→        "        la      r0,_main",
    13→        "        jal     r1,(r0)",
    14→        "_halt:",
    15→        "        bra     _halt",
    16→    );
    17→}
